segments.s246-g73: درفشی درفشان is a noun phrase meaning ‘a (shining) banner/standard’; درفشان is adjectival ‘shining, glittering’. پس پشت اوی is an adverbial phrase ‘behind him’ (پس = ‘after/behind’, پشت = ‘rear/behind’) with اوی the archaic possessive/pronoun ‘his’. Word order is typical older Persian: descriptive noun phrase first, then a positional phrase indicating location relative to the main figure.
segments.s246-g74: این عبارت is a short nominal clause: یکی زابلی ‘a certain/one from Zabul’ (یکی as ‘someone/a certain’) followed by the locative/instrumental phrase تیغ در مشت اوی ‘a sword in his fist’ where در = ‘in’ and مشت اوی = ‘his fist/hand’ (possessive expressed by postposed اوی). The order Noun + prepositional phrase is normal in classical Persian.
segments.s246-g75: This clause reports perception: غو ‘uproar/noise’ is the event, دیده (past participle ‘seen’) together with بشنید (‘heard’) form a paired perception expression, and دستان سام literally ‘the hands of Sam’ (here ‘Sam’s men/forces’) is the agent that perceived. The old poetic order places the event word first, then the verbal perception phrase, then the possessor-marked noun.
segments.s246-g76: بفرمود is the honorific/simple past ‘he commanded/said’. بر here introduces the to‑infinitive-like construction (‘to/that … be done’) and چرمه کردن لگام is the verbal nominal phrase ‘to put the bridle on the hide’ (چرمه = hide/leather, کردن as the light/auxiliary verb forming the verbal peri‑phrase, لگام = bridle). The whole means ‘he ordered that the bridle be put on the hide’.
segments.s246-g77: پراندیشه آمد is an adjectival/nominal phrase plus past verb: پراندیشه ‘thoughtful, anxious’ modifies the coming (آمد). پذیره براه is an older expression meaning ‘to receive/meet on the road’ or ‘came to welcome on the road’ (پذیره here functioning as a reception/meeting noun/participial sense, براه = ‘on the road’). The sequence is typical: descriptor + verb + purpose/location.
segments.s246-g78: بدان is a linking compound here (ب + دان) functioning like ‘by this/with that’ or ‘so’ and introduces the purpose clause begun by تا ‘so that/in order that’. نباشد is the negative subjunctive ‘may not be’ (classical negative desire/possibility). یکی کینه خواه is a noun phrase ‘a single/any one who seeks vengeance’ (کینه‌خواه = ‘one who wants revenge’). Overall: ‘...so that there be no one desiring vengeance.’
segments.s246-g79: ز ره = ‘from the road’ (ز = poetic ‘from’, ره = ‘way/road’). گیو را دید = ‘Giv saw him’ (را marks the definite object). پژمرده روی is an adjectival phrase placed after the verb: پژمرده ‘pale/withered’ modifies روی ‘face’, so ‘with a pale/withered face’. The clause order (prepositional phrase → subject → را → verb → adjectival phrase) is normal in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s246-g80: همی آمد is the imperfective/continuous past sense (‘was coming’ / ‘came repeatedly’), where همی is the classical continuous marker. آسیمه is an adjective/adverb ‘distraught/hurried’; و = ‘and’; پوی پوی is a reduplicated adverbial ‘swift/fast, rushing about’ (the repetition intensifies the meaning). Together: ‘he came distraught and hurrying, swiftly, hither and thither.’
segments.s246-g225: همه دست is the subject phrase 'all hands / everyone’s hands'. There is no overt copula (بودند) — a common poetic omission — so لعل functions as a predicative adjective 'ruby'. از introduces the source ('from / because of'), and می‌لعل فام is a compound attributive 'ruby-hued wine' (می‌لعل as the ruby-colored wine and فام ‘hue’ qualifying it). Word order is subject + predicative phrase.
segments.s246-g226: غریونده and خروشنده are present-participial/adjectival forms meaning 'roaring/resounding' and they modify the following nouns. Here the poet places the participial adjective before the noun (غریونده چنگ = 'the harp, roaring') and links two parallel phrases with و. There is again no finite verb; the clause gives concurrent sound-images.
segments.s246-g227: بروز چهارم is a temporal adverbial 'on the fourth day' (بروز = 'on the day / day'), placed before the verb. گرفتند is past-tense plural 'they took/ seized' and ساز is the object 'instruments' (object follows the verb).
segments.s246-g228: چو here means 'when'; آمدش is a contracted verbal phrase 'it came to him/it came' (آمد + clitic ش), so 'when the moment came to him'. هنگام رفتن فراز is a nominal time-expression 'the time of going/ departure for (the) ascent' — هنگام governs the gerund رفتن, and فراز ('ascent / on high') characterizes the act of mounting/departing. Together: 'when the time to depart/mount came.'
segments.s246-g229: بفرمود is a polite/past reporting verb 'he ordered/said'. It is followed by a subordinate clause introduced by که. بندید here is a jussive/subjunctive form used in reported commands ('that they bind / tie up'), and بار is the direct object 'baggage / packs'. The clause thus means 'Rostam ordered that the packs be tied.'
segments.s246-g230: سوی شاه ایران is a prepositional phrase 'toward the King of Iran' (سوی = toward(s)). بسیچید is a past-tense verb 'they mustered/assembled / marshaled' and takes کار ('the work/affairs') as its object; the sequence means they set about organizing/preparing the business toward the king.
segments.s246-g231: سواران گردنکش is a noun + adjectival epithet: 'the horsemen, headstrong / rebellious' (گردنکش modifies سواران). از کشورش is a prepositional phrase 'from his country' where ش is the possessive clitic 'his' attached to کشور.
segments.s246-g232: همه راه را is a definite object phrase 'the whole road' marked by the object marker را. ساخته is a past participle / perfect-past 'made / prepared / arrayed' used here to mean 'had lined/ stationned (the road)'. بر درش is a locative 'at/on his gate' (بر = at/toward, در = gate/door, ش = his). Together: 'had lined the whole road at his gate.'
segments.s246-g169: وز آنجا بایوان رستم شدند — This is a compact, somewhat archaic locative clause. وز (here written together) functions like a poetic preposition 'و ز' = 'and/from there' introducing departure; آنجا = 'there'; بایوان is an architectural noun (iwan/portico) but here the exact form is old/uncertain; رستم is the proper name placed after the place (iwan) to indicate possession/association; شدند is the past plural of شدن used here to indicate movement/arrival/coming into that place (classical usage of شدن can mark change of location). Word order is place + possessor + verb, typical of older Persian narrative.
segments.s246-g170: بره بر همی رای رفتن زدند — This clause describes the action that follows: بر (here) is the prepositional 'on/upon', همی (classical particle) emphasizes mutuality or continuative sense tied to رای (plan/counsel); رای = 'counsel/plan'; رفتن is the verbal noun 'to go', and زدند (past plural of زدن) combines with the infinitive to mean 'they struck to go' i.e. 'they set out / began to go' — a common periphrasis in older Persian where زدن + infinitive indicates commencement of the action. The first word بره appears in the manuscript-form here and its meaning is uncertain in this context (see senses).
segments.s246-g171: چو آن نامه شاه رستم بخواند — چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'; آن نامه = 'that letter' (object phrase); شاه رستم is the genitival sequence 'the king (i.e. the royal) — Rostam' (here 'شاه' qualifies the letter as royal and 'رستم' names the addressee or possessor as per context); بخواند is the past tense form meaning 'read' (classical perfective). The clause is a temporal subordinate: 'when Rostam read that letter.'
segments.s246-g172: ز گفتار خسرو بخیره بماند — ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of' introducing cause; گفتار خسرو = 'Khosrow's words/speech' (genitive construction); بخیره is an adverbial classical form meaning something like 'in a state (often of surprise or fortunate preservation)' (here it marks the manner/state in which he remained); بماند is past tense 'he remained' (stative). Together: 'he remained (there) because of / in response to Khosrow's words' (i.e. struck/astonished by them).
segments.s246-g173: ز بس آفرین جهاندار شاه — ز بس = 'because of so much' or 'from so great'; آفرین here is a noun 'praise/acclamation' (classical usage); جهاندار شاه is an honorific compound 'the world‑holder king' (an epithet for the sovereign). Grammatically this is a causal phrase: 'owing to the king's great praise...'.
segments.s246-g174: بد آن نامه‌بر پهلوان سپاه — بد here is an exclamatory/pejorative interjection 'bad/woe'; آن = 'that'; نامه‌بر = 'letter‑carrier, envoy' (the person who bore the letter); پهلوان سپاه = 'champion of the host/army' (noun phrase in apposition). The phrase expresses condemnation or misfortune directed at that bearer: 'woe to that letter‑carrier, the army's champion.'
segments.s246-g175: بگیو آنگهی گفت بشناختم — بگیو is an archaic/formal reporting particle meaning 'he said' or an imperative 'say' used in narrative; آنگهی = 'then/thereupon'; گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (here part of the narration); بشناختم is the first person past 'I recognized / I came to know'. In sequence this reads as reported speech: 'He said then, "I understood/learned..."'.
segments.s246-g176: بفرمان او راه را ساختم — بفرمان = 'by order of' (prepositional phrase); او = 'he' (the person who gave the order); راه = 'road/way' (direct object); را marks the definite object; ساختم is the first person past 'I made / I built'. Together: 'By his command I made the road.' The clause closes the reported speech with an agentive result of the order.
segments.s246-g41: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('that'). هرگز is an adverb of negation ('never'). بدین دودمان functions as the indirect/affected phrase 'this dynasty' (here written contracted and meaning 'to/for this dynasty'); the negated predicate follows: غم نبود = 'there was no sorrow' (نبود is the negative past copula/existential ‘was not’). Word order is typical classical Persian: adverb + indirect phrase + predicate.
segments.s246-g42: فروزنده‌تر is a comparative adjective 'more illuminating/more radiant'. زین here works as a comparative prepositional phrase 'than this' (classical usage 'زِ این'); چنانکم is a relative/comparative 'such as / that which', and شنود is the third-person verb 'he hears/heard' (classical form). Together the clause says 'more illuminating than that which he heard.'
segments.s246-g43: نبد is the negative/existential 'there was not / did not exist'. گیو را places Giv as the topical/affected person (را used as topic/emphasis here). خود 'of his own' emphasizes possession. جز introduces the exception 'except', followed by این پور کس 'this son (of his) person' = 'this son'. The construction means 'Giv had no one of his own except this son.'
segments.s246-g44: The repeated چه is exclamatory here ('what a...!'). فرزند بود is a predicative clause 'was a son' (بود = was). The second چه before فریادرس intensifies 'what a rescuer he was' (فریادرس = helper/rescuer). The two parts form an exclamation praising the son.
segments.s246-g45: فراوان is an adjective/adverb 'many' or 'abundantly'. بنزد (classical form of 'به نزد') means 'near / in the presence of'. منش دستگاه likely forms a genitive/attributive phrase 'the household/court (منش = character/origin, دستگاه = court/establishment)', i.e. 'in the court/establishment'. The phrase serves to locate those who were many.
segments.s246-g46: مرا و نیای مرا are definite phrases marked by را ('me and my forefather/ancestor') and are the objects/beneficiaries of the predication. نیکخواه is a predicate/adjective 'well-wisher' or 'well-disposed'. The full sense: 'many (people) near the court were well-wishers of me and of my ancestor.' The verb copula is omitted (understood) as is common in classical verse.
segments.s246-g47: بهر سو = 'to whatever side/quarter' (بهر = toward/for, سو = side/direction). که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/where' and جویمش is 'I seek him' (جویم + enclitic -ش = 'him'). یابم is a subjunctive/optative form 'may I find' (expressing a wish). بجای 'in place / in his place' qualifies where one finds him. The clause expresses a wish: 'whichever way I go to seek him, may I find him (there).'
segments.s246-g48: بهر نیک و بد = 'for good and bad' i.e. 'whether for good or ill'. پیش من = 'before me / in my presence'. بربپای is a compound prepositional phrase meaning 'at (or upon) the feet' (i.e. 'at my feet' — a formulaic image of submission or close presence). The whole final clause specifies the hoped-for result: 'may I find him before me at my feet, whether for good or ill.'
segments.s246-g241: که here is the subordinating conjunction meaning 'because/that', introducing the reason. نابردنی is an adjective formed with na- (negation) + بردنی (from بردن) meaning 'unable to be taken / unconquerable' and is predicated with the past copula بود (here past 'was'). برگاشتند is the main past verb 'they returned/withdrew' (3rd pl.), so the clause reads literally 'that (it) was unconquerable, they returned' with the plural subject expressed by the verb ending -ند.
segments.s246-g242: This is a short clause with two names as direct objects followed by the verb. بزال و فرامرز are coordinated by و ('and'); the verb بگذاشتند (past 3rd pl.) here means 'they left (behind)' rather than 'placed'. The implied subject is the same 'they' as in the previous verb, so the sequence means 'they left Bazal and Faramarz (behind)'.
segments.s246-g243: سوی is the preposition 'toward', followed by the noun phrase شهر ایران 'the city of Iran'. نهادند is the past tense 'they placed/set'; روی ('face') is the object being set, so نهادند روی means 'they set (their) face'. Poetic word order places the directional phrase up front: 'toward the city of Iran they set (their) face.'
segments.s246-g244: This is an appositive/descriptor of those who act: همه ('all') modifies راه پویان, where پویان is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'traveling/active' applied to راه ('road'), i.e. 'all who traverse the roads.' دل کینه‌جوی uses دل ('heart') with the adjective کینه‌جوی ('vengeful' / 'revenge-seeking'), a common Persian idiom meaning 'with vengeful hearts.'
segments.s246-g245: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. رستم is the subject. بنزدیک is an adverb/locative meaning 'near' (literally 'at-nearness') placed before ایران ('Iran') to mean 'near Iran'. رسید is the past tense 'arrived/reached' (3rd sing.), so the clause reads 'when Rostam reached/approached near Iran.'
segments.s246-g246: This repeats the verb structure to specify a nearer location: بنزدیک 'near' + شهر دلیران 'the city of the brave' and رسید 'he arrived'. The subject remains Rostam; the second رسید confirms he came close to that particular city.
segments.s246-g247: یکی is the indefinite 'one / a certain' here introducing the noun. باد نوشین is a noun + adjective ('a sweet/delightful breeze') with the adjective after the noun in poetic classical order. درود سپهر is a genitive compound 'the greeting of the sky/heavens' (درود possessed by سپهر), so the whole phrase describes 'a certain sweet breeze — the heavens' greeting.'
segments.s246-g248: برستم here is the prepositional/benefactive phrase 'to/for Rostam' (بر + رستم). رسانید is the past tense verb 'conveyed/delivered' (the wind as subject). شادان 'joyful' and بمهر 'with affection / with love' are manner/attitude phrases describing how the greeting was delivered. Note: the lemma list's senses for برستم do not match this usage, so its sense is marked below as unmapped.
segments.s246-g161: پس (then) + از بهر (lit. 'from/for the sake of') introduces the motive: an archaic prepositional phrase meaning 'for the sake of Bijan.' خروشید is a past-tense, third-person verb 'cried out/roared,' and زار is an adverbial adjective 'lamenting/woeful' modifying the manner of the cry. Word order is typical of classical Persian: temporal/motivating phrase → subject/actor implied → verb → manner.
segments.s246-g162: فرو ریخت is a compound verbal expression meaning 'poured downward' (فرو as directional prefix + ریخت 'poured'), here in past tense. از دیده marks the source 'from the eyes,' and خون is the object 'blood.' برکنار here functions adverbially, indicating direction/position ('aside/away/downward from the eye'); the phrase describes blood streaming from the eyes.
segments.s246-g163: بگیو is an archaic imperative/hortative 'say!' (classical form of بگو), followed by آنگهی 'then/thereupon.' گفت functions as the reporting verb 'he said.' مندیش ازین together express 'do not suppose/think this' or 'do not be mistaken about this' (مندیش is a negative or cautionary notion here and ازین = از این 'of/about this'). The sequence is an introductory formula to the reported clause.
segments.s246-g164: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. رستم is the subject. نگرداند is a negative perfective/preterite form (ن‑ + گرداند) meaning 'would not / did not cause to be turned / did not remove' (here: 'not take off/remove'). از رخش زین is an object phrase where از marks separation 'from his steed (رخش) the saddle (زین).' The clause states the action Rostam did not perform (i.e., removing the saddle from the steed).
segments.s246-g165: مگر here functions adversatively ('but' / 'however'), introducing a contrast. دست بیژن گرفته is a compact passive/affected expression: 'Bijan's hand was taken' or 'they seized Bijan' (گرفته as past participle 'seized'), and بدست emphasizes the manner/place ('into the hand' / 'by hand' = 'seized'). The clause conveys that Bijan was captured.
segments.s246-g166: همه lists the extent ('entirely/all'), بند و زندان are the instruments/conditions 'fetters and prison.' او کرده پست is a causative/resultative sequence: کرده (made/done) + پست (low/despicable) → 'they made him low/demeaned him.' Word order places the objects (بند و زندان) before the result clause.
segments.s246-g167: بنیروی is a prepositional phrase 'by the power of' (بِ + نیروی); یزدان is the divine agent 'God.' و فرمان شاه links 'and the king's command' as a second source of authority. The whole segment gives the authorities invoked for the speaker's action.
segments.s246-g168: ز (poetic form of از) marks source 'from Turan.' بگردانم is first-person singular present/subjunctive/future meaning 'I will turn/restore/return' (the verbal form expresses the speaker's resolve). این تاج و گاه is the direct object ('this crown and seat/throne'); in classical usage گاه can denote a seat/place. Word order places the verb before its object in this poetic line.
segments.s246-g97: شوم گفت is a subject (a proper name, here the speaker) plus the past verb 'said'. تا introduces a purpose clause ('so that'); من is the subject of the embedded clause; ببینمش is the present/subjunctive form بِبینم ('that I may see') with the object clitic -ش 'him/her' attached, so 'that I may see him'; روی is the direct object 'face'.
segments.s246-g98: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of'; خسرو is the name Khosrow; یکی نامه literally 'one (a certain) letter' (یکی = a certain/one); دارم is the present 'I have' (I possess); بدوی does not match the provided senses in this context and appears to function as a target/recipient clitic or an enclitic phrase ('for him' / 'to him') in the line, so its listed senses are not applicable.
segments.s246-g99: بدو is the prepositional/adverbial phrase 'to him' (or 'unto him'); گفت functions as the reporting verb 'said'; دستان here is a vocative or address (the word's common listed senses 'hands/tales' do not fit neatly — treat it as an address or name in context); کز is the relative/conjunction 'that/so that/which' used here to introduce the clause; ایدر is the adverb 'here'; مرو is the negative imperative 'do not go' (مَرو = don't go).
segments.s246-g100: که is the conjunction 'for/that' introducing reason or explanatory clause; زود is the adverb 'soon/quickly'; آید is the present/future-oriented verb 'will come' (here simple present used as near future); از is the preposition 'from'; دشت is 'plain' (open ground); نخچیرگو names the huntsman or game‑dealer — here functioning as the agent who will arrive.
segments.s246-g101: تو is the vocative/second‑person pronoun 'you'; تا with آید ('until Rostam comes') sets a temporal limit ('until'); رستم آید is 'Rostam comes' (آید is present used for near future); بخانه is the compound prepositional phrase 'to/at the house' (بـ + خانه); بپای is a verbal compound meaning 'remain/abide/wait' (بـ + پای, here 'stay/wait').
segments.s246-g102: یک امروز literally 'one day today' (emphatic 'this one day' / 'for one day'); با ما 'with us'; بشادی is an adverbial phrase 'in joy, merrily' (بـ + شادی); گرای is here a vocative or proper-name/form of address (its listed senses do not match this function), so it likely addresses someone or is a name-like exclamation.
segments.s246-g103: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; گیو is the proper name Giv; اندر آمد is the past tense 'came in/entered' (اندر = in/within + آمد = came); بایوان in context denotes movement into the iwan/portico (the provided lemma senses are uncertain/archaic, so treat this as a locative/goal phrase meaning 'into the iwan'); ز راه 'from the road' (ز = from, راه = road).
segments.s246-g104: تهمتن is an epithet/name meaning 'Tahmātan' or 'the mighty one' (used as a proper name here); بیامد is the past 'came' (بـ + آمد or literary past form); ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; نخچیرگاه is 'hunting‑ground' (the place whence he came).
segments.s246-g65: بیابان گرفت: a common Persian idiom meaning 'took to the wilderness' — بیابان (object) = 'the desert/wilderness' and گرفت is past-tense 'took' (from گرفتن used here as 'take to/enter'). The conjunction و simply links this phrase to what follows. ره هیرمند = 'the road/route to Helmand' with ره (way) followed by the proper name هیرمند.
segments.s246-g66: همی رفت پویان بسان نوند: همی is the older progressive particle 'still/was' marking continuous action; رفت is simple past 'went/rode'; پویان here is a participial/adverbial description 'moving/active' (describing the motion); بسان = 'like/as'; نوند is a name/term (here used as the point of comparison). The whole phrase describes continuous movement likened to 'Navand'.
segments.s246-g67: بکوه و بصحرا نهادند روی: بکوه and بصحرا are directional phrases 'to the mountain' and 'to the plain' (بـ- prefix + noun). نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past of نهادن 'to place/turn (one's face)', here 'they turned/set'; روی = 'face' used idiomatically as 'their faces' or 'toward'. The clause states they directed themselves toward mountain and plain.
segments.s246-g68: همی شد خلیده دل و راه‌جوی: همی again marks continuous/resultative aspect 'became'; شد is 'became'; خلیده is an adjective 'secluded/withdrawn' describing دل 'heart' (دل = heart); و links to راه‌جوی, a compound meaning 'seeker of the road' or 'traveler/pursuer'. Grammatically this is a small resultant clause: 'their hearts became secluded and they became road-seekers.'
segments.s246-g69: چو از دیده‌گه دیده‌بانش بدید: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction); از + دیده‌گه = 'from the lookout/field of vision' (دیده‌گه = vantage/lookout); دیده‌بانش is دیده‌بان 'watchman/sentinel' with the possessive suffix -ش 'his' (so 'his watchman'); بدید is simple past 'saw'. Order is typical older Persian: temporal conjunction + source/place + subject + verb.
segments.s246-g70: سوی زابلستان فغان برکشید: سوی = 'toward(s)'; زابلستان is the place-name 'Zabulistan'; فغان = 'a cry/lament' (object); برکشید is past 'raised/lifted' (here 'lifted/cried out a lament'). The verb takes فغان as its object — 'he raised a cry toward Zabulistan.'
segments.s246-g71: که آمد سواری سوی هیرمند: که here introduces the clause (often 'that' or 'when'); آمد is simple past 'came'; سواری = 'a rider, horseman' (subject); سوی + هیرمند = 'toward Helmand'. The clause states that a rider approached Helmand.
segments.s246-g72: سواران بگرد اندرش نیز چند: سواران = 'horsemen' (subject). بگرد here is an older verbal form meaning 'circulated/turned about' (imperative-form look in the lexicon but here functions narratively, 'turned/rode about'); اندر + ش = 'around/within him' with ش the 3rd-person possessive pronoun 'his' (so 'around him'); نیز = 'also/likewise'; چند = 'several/a few'. Together: 'several horsemen likewise rode/turned around him.'
segments.s246-g209: برو آفرین کرد is a verbal phrase in which آفرین ('praise, “bravo”') is treated as the thing uttered and کرد is the past-tense verb 'did / uttered'. The initial برو here is idiomatic/exclamatory in the older poetic register and combines with آفرین to indicate an outcry of praise; کای نامور is a vocative: کای is the archaic vocative 'O' (used for kings) and نامور modifies it as 'renowned'.
segments.s246-g210: This is a coordinated list of blessings/attributes introduced without an explicit verb (the verb 'may you have' is supplied by the preceding clause). بمردی is an abstract/nominal form meaning 'manhood / manliness', and the remaining nouns نیروی, بخت, هنر are simple nouns joined by و meaning 'and' (strength, fortune, skill).
segments.s246-g211: بماناد is an optative/ desiderative 3rd-person form 'may [it] remain' (old poetic ending -اد). بر تو 'upon you/for you' marks the beneficiary, while چنین جاودان 'such eternal' describes the wished-for state. Word order puts the optative verb first, followed by its beneficiary and modifiers.
segments.s246-g212: A noun series giving the content of the wish. تن پیل is a compound-like expression 'body of an elephant' (elephant-sized/body like an elephant) and هوش و دل موبدان lists two attributes: 'intellect' and 'heart' where موبدان (mōbedān) is in apposition/genitive to دل (the heart of the mobeds), i.e. priestly wisdom/heart.
segments.s246-g213: ز هر نیکی بهره‌ور is a wish: ز ('in' / 'with respect to') هر نیکی 'every goodness' and بهره‌ور 'possessing a share / prosperous / endowed'. The final element بادیا here functions as an archaic optative/exclamatory fusion meaning 'may he be' ( باد = 'may be' in older usage, with یا as an exclamatory particle), so the whole clause is a benediction.
segments.s246-g214: چنین introduces the result clause 'so that', and کز is the subordinating 'that/from which'. دلم is 'my heart' (دل + possessive -م). The clitic م is listed separately here as an archaic existential/copula element 'is / there is' linking subject and predicate. زنگ 'ringing / bell/alarum' is figurative for a stirring in the heart, and بزدادیا is an old-form verb meaning 'increase' (here optative: 'may increase').
segments.s246-g215: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. رستم is the subject and دید the past tense 'saw / beheld'. دل گیو پدرام functions as the object phrase: دل 'heart' with گیو (the name Giv) as possessor; پدرام appears as an appositive or additional name/epithet linked to گیو — in poetry personal names often appear in sequence as identifiers.
segments.s246-g216: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial 'thereupon / after that'. بنیکی is an archaic adverbial/phrase meaning 'with goodness / in a good way' and سرانجام 'in the end / as an outcome' modifies دید 'he saw' — together giving 'he saw a good outcome' or 'a favourable end followed'.
segments.s246-g153: ترا is the object clitic (te + the object marker را) placed before the verb, so 'you' is the object of دیدم. دیدم is 1st-person past ('I saw'). اندر جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world'. چاره‌گر is a predicative noun ('solver, one who finds a remedy') used after the verb phrase with an implicit copula: 'I saw you (to be) the solver in the world.'
segments.s246-g154: تو is the explicit subject pronoun 'you'. بندی is the 2nd-person singular present form (stem + -ی) meaning 'you bind/gird' (classical Persian uses this present to express habitual or immediate action). بفریاد functions like a prepositional/participial element 'to the aid of' (i.e. 'in aid of'), and هر کس کمر means 'the loins/waist of each/any person' (کمر = loins/waist). Word order is literary: verb (بندی) before the object phrase ('بفریاد هر کس کمر').
segments.s246-g155: همی here is the habitual/continuative particle 'kept/was still'. گفت is a past verb 'he said/was saying' (literary presentive past). و links to the following image. مژگان 'eyelashes' + پر (used as 'full (of)') + از آب 'with water' (tears) + زرد 'yellow/pale' — the phrase means 'his lashes full of yellow/pale water' (i.e. tear-filled lashes).
segments.s246-g156: همی again marks continuous action. برکشید is past (3rd sing.) 'he lifted/raised' (here of the lashes or of the head). از introduces source 'from/out of'. جگر is used metaphorically as 'heart/seat of feeling' rather than the literal liver. باد سرد is a noun phrase 'a cold wind/breeze' that 'rose' from the heart; the sequence reads 'a cold breeze rose from the heart.'
segments.s246-g157: ازان پس که is a temporal clause 'after that, when'. نامه is 'the letter'. برستم is an archaic/literary verbal form here meaning 'I (or he) took up/handed/produced' the letter (classical usage; the precise lexical entry in the provided list is imperfect). بداد appears where one would expect a past action 'gave/delivered' (in modern Persian داد or بداد); grammatically this cluster expresses the action of giving/handing the letter after that time.
segments.s246-g158: همه کار = 'all matters' (همه = all/entire; کار = affairs). گرگین is the proper name Gorgin here (not the adjective 'wolf-like'). بدو is 'to him' (dative/adverbial). کرد یاد is a light-verb construction: کرد 'did/made' + یاد 'remembrance' = 'made (him) remember' or simply 'reminded (him)'. So the clause means 'he reminded Gorgin of all matters' or 'he made Gorgin recall all his affairs.'
segments.s246-g159: ازو is an ablative/genitive 'from him' or 'concerning him'. نامه بستد is past tense 'he shut/sealed the letter' (بستد = closed/sealed). دو دیده پر از آب is a compact phrase 'both eyes full of water' (دو = two/both; دیده = eyes; پر از = full of; آب = water/tears). The verb بستد is 3rd-singular past and the clause describes the action immediately followed by the physical image of tear-filled eyes.
segments.s246-g160: همه دل = 'the whole heart' (همه intensifies دل). پر از کین = 'full of hatred/vengeance' (پر از = full of; کین = hatred/vengeance). افراسیاب at the end is the possessor: 'Afrasiab's whole heart was full of hatred.' In Persian the possessor frequently follows the possessed noun phrase in this poetic word order.
segments.s246-g289: بپرسید is a past third-person plural form 'asked' (classical/poetic). The object pronoun مر ('me', an older form of مرا) immediately follows the verb. هریکی را literally 'each one' is placed after the verb and bears را here as a topical/emphatic marker (common in poetry) rather than as a plain direct object; ز شاه ('about/from the king') is a short prepositional phrase indicating the topic of the questioning.
segments.s246-g290: ز introduces the following descriptive phrase 'about' or 'concerning'. گردنده is a present‑participle/adjectival form 'turning' or 'revolving' modifying خورشید ('sun'); و coordinates the second epithet; تابنده is another participle/adjective 'shining, resplendent' modifying ماه ('moon'). Together the phrase supplies epithets linked to 'شاه' in the previous segment.
segments.s246-g291: نشستند is the past third‑person plural 'they sat down/they took their seats' (verb precedes subject here, as is common in epic diction). گردان و رستم is the coordinated subject 'the troop and Rostam'. بر اسب is a prepositional phrase 'on (a/the) horse' indicating where they sat.
segments.s246-g292: بکردار is an adjectival phrase 'of good form/virtuous/handsome' modifying the following noun; رخشنده 'radiant' is a coordinating adjective; آذرگشسب is a proper name used here after its modifiers (the name of the steed/person), so the whole phrase apposes or identifies the horse mentioned in the previous segment.
segments.s246-g233: بیامد (past) is the main past verb 'he came/approached.' به رخش is a prepositional phrase 'to his steed' (به + رخ + ـش = to his horse). اندر آورد پای is a poetic, slightly inverted construction: اندر آورد = 'brought/put in' and پای = '(his) foot' — together 'he put his foot in (i.e. into the stirrup).' Word order (preposition + possessor attached to رخ) and the verbal phrase اندر آورد follow classical Persian poetic inversion.
segments.s246-g234: کمر بست is a fixed expression 'he girded his loins / bound his waist' — کمر = waist and بست is the past 'bound/tied.' و links the two actions. پوشید is past 'he put on / donned,' and رومی قبای is the object 'a Roman/Byzantine robe' (رومی qualifying قبای). The sequence lists consecutive past actions.
segments.s246-g235: بزین here is the compressed classical form (literally 'بر زین') indicating location 'on/to the saddle.' اندر افگند is the past verb phrase 'he threw into/on (it),' with گرز 'mace/club' as the direct object. نیا follows as an epithet 'ancestral' or 'of the ancestor' qualifying the mace; in poetic usage it names the mace as belonging to the forebears.
segments.s246-g236: This line uses inverted, parallel phrases: 'پر از جنگ سر' = 'a head full of war' and 'دل پر از کیمیا' = 'a heart full of kimia.' پر از + noun means 'full of (X)'; the noun (سر or دل) is placed after the phrase for poetic effect, so the literal order is inverted compared with modern prose. کیمیا here is figurative (some secret/transformative art), so 'full of alchemical cunning' or 'full of secret art' is the sense.
segments.s246-g237: بگردون is a poetic form of 'to turn/make turn' (here past action 'he turned/moved'). برافراخته is a participle/adjective 'raised, erect' modifying گوش 'ear' (گوش برافراخته = 'with ears raised'). رخش is the possessor 'his steed.' The verb + participle + object order is typical in epic diction.
segments.s246-g238: ز خورشید برتر سر is a poetic inverted comparative: literally 'from the sun higher (is) the head' → 'whose head is higher than the sun.' ز functions as the comparative particle here. تاج‌بخش is an appositive epithet 'crown-giver' (a title/attribute), placed after سر to describe that lofty head. Poetic inversion and apposition are in play.
segments.s246-g239: خود و گیو is a coordinated subject 'he himself and Giv.' با زابلی adds 'with a Zābli' (an attendant from Zabul). صد سوار (here as a noun phrase) is the quantity and class 'a hundred horsemen' associated with that subject; the verb selecting them appears in the next segment. The phrase lists the people who took part in the selection.
segments.s246-g240: ز لشکر گزید is the past action 'he selected/chose from the host' — ز لشکر is ablative 'from the army' and گزید is the past of 'to choose.' از در کارزار is a locational/intent phrase: در کارزار = 'in/for the battlefield' and the preceding از ties it as 'from (those) for the field of battle' or 'to send into the field.' The overall sense: they picked a hundred horsemen out of the army to take into the battle.
segments.s246-g177: بدانستم is a 1st-person past verb 'I knew/realised'. The following noun phrase 'این رنج و کردار تو' is its direct content: این 'this' + رنج 'suffering' coordinated with کردار 'conduct/behavior', finished by the possessive تو 'your'. Word order is demonstrative + head nouns + possessor.
segments.s246-g178: کشیدن here is a verbal noun/infinitive meaning 'to bear/endure' and functions as the predicate/content of بدانستم (i.e. 'that (you) endured'). بهر is the preposition 'for/for the sake of', followed by the noun phrase 'کار تیمار تو' = 'the matter/effort of caring for you'. The final تو attaches to تیمار ('your care').
segments.s246-g179: چه مایه ترا is an interrogative formula 'what reason/motive (have) you' — چه 'what' + مایه 'cause/reason' + the enclitic object/oblique ترا (ت + را). نزد من places the action 'in my presence / at my court', and دستگاه follows as 'a device/scheme' or 'establishment' located there.
segments.s246-g180: بهر introduces purpose 'for/for the sake of' and governs کینه‌گاه 'seat/den of hatred'; اندرون 'within/inside' qualifies that place, and کینه خواه is a compound meaning 'desiring/seekng hatred' — خواه is the present stem 'wants/desires' used here as a verb/participial element.
segments.s246-g181: The paired چه ... چه introduces alternatives ('whether ... or ...'). کین 'grudge/vengeance' modifies the following names: سیاوش and مازندران; the construction names the objects/agents of hostility.
segments.s246-g182: کمر بسته is a past-participial adjectival phrase 'having bound the waist' = idiomatically 'girded their loins / prepared themselves'. بر پیش indicates position 'before/against' and is followed by جنگاوران 'warriors' as the reference group. The verb is implicit; the phrase predicates readiness.
segments.s246-g183: برین is the classical compound 'بر + این' meaning 'to/on this' and with آمدن (the gerund/infinitive 'coming') forms a nominal clause 'coming to this/coming here'. رنج برداشتی is a finite past clause 'you bore/endured hardship' (برداشتی = 'you took/bore').
segments.s246-g184: چنین 'thus/so' links the result; راه دشوار is the object phrase 'a difficult path', and بگذاشتی is a 2nd-person past verb 'you set/placed/established' (here 'you put in place / you made'). The clause states the consequence of the coming and the endured hardship.
segments.s246-g281: بر آیین کاوس forms an adverbial prepositional phrase placed before the verb: بر + آیین = 'in accordance with / according to' (poetic usage). کاوس is the name being specified by آیین. برخاستند is past simple, 3rd person plural 'they rose' (verb at the end as in Persian word order).
segments.s246-g282: پذیره شدن را is a nominalized passive phrase: پذیره (reception) + شدن (to be / passive) = 'the being received / the reception', and را marks it as the definite direct object. بیاراستند is past 3rd pl. 'they arranged / adorned / prepared' (بیاراستن = to set in order, here 'prepare the reception').
segments.s246-g283: جهان شد is a simple past/result clause 'the world became / was made'. ز + گرد + سواران is a poetic source/agent phrase: ز (from/of) + گرد (around / a ring/circle) + سواران (horsemen) = 'from the ring of horsemen' i.e. 'surrounded by / filled with a ring of horsemen'. بنفش is a postposed color adjective modifying سواران (poetic placement after the noun).
segments.s246-g284: This is a pair of coordinated adjective + noun phrases: درخشان سنان = 'spears (سنان) shining' and درفشان درفش = 'the banner (درفش) glittering/flashingly bright' (درفشان here is the adjective 'shining'). The conjunction و links them; the adjective sometimes appears before the noun in poetry for metrical or emphatic reasons.
segments.s246-g285: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نزدیک رستم is a prepositional/locative phrase 'near Rostam'. فراز آمدند is a compound verbal phrase where فراز (on high / upwards) gives the sense 'came up / advanced' and آمدند is past 3rd pl. 'they came'. Word order places the verb at the end as usual in Persian.
segments.s246-g286: پیاده is an adverb/adjective 'on foot'. برسم نماز آمدند is purposive: برسم (from رسیدن/رس = 'to arrive / come to') + نماز (prayer) + آمدند = 'they came to (perform) the prayer' — so the clause means 'they arrived on foot in order to pray.' The verb آمدند repeats the past action; the construction indicates purpose/arrival for the prayer.
segments.s246-g287: ز اسب اندر آمد is a compact verbal phrase meaning 'he came down from the horse / dismounted': ز (from) + اسب (horse) + اندر آمد (came down/entered down) — اندر آمد is an older/poetic way to express dismounting. جهان‌پهلوان (world-champion) is the subject; in this poetic word order the subject follows the verb.
segments.s246-g288: کجا introduces a rhetorical question ('how / where'); پهلوانان is 'the champions' (subject). بپیشش is a prepositional/adverbial form meaning 'in his presence / before him' (بپیش + ش). نوان is an archaic/poetic form here (not one of the modern listed senses) functioning as a verbal element meaning something like 'could / had power' (i.e. 'how could the champions [compare / act] before him?').
segments.s246-g257: Postpositional phrase + clause: 'پس از' = 'after' (پس here with از makes a temporal 'after'); 'گیو' is the noun governed by that preposition. Then the core clause 'گودرز پرسید شاه' has subject گودرز, verb پرسید (he asked) and شاه as the person asked (direct object/indirect object in older word order). The whole segment reads: 'After Giv, Goudarz asked the king...'.
segments.s246-g258: Indirect question introduced by 'که' = 'that/which' used here to mark the content of the question. 'رستم کجا ماند' = 'where Rostam remained/was left' (کجا = where; ماند = remained). 'چون بود راه' is a follow-up question clause: 'چون' = 'how/what sort of', 'بود' = past of 'to be', 'راه' = 'road/route' — together 'how was the road'. The clauses form the content of what was asked.
segments.s246-g259: 'بدو' here is the dative/enclitic 'to him' (= 'to that one'); 'گفت' is the verb 'said' (the list gives noun senses but context is verbal 'he said'). 'گیو‌ای' is the vocative form 'Giv—O Giv' (ای marks address). 'شه نامدار' is a nominal epithet 'famed lord/king' placed after the vocative as an address.
segments.s246-g260: 'برآید' is a 3rd-person-form used optatively/subjunctively here — 'may it arise/occur'. The phrase 'ببخت تو' is an archaic contraction (literally 'to/for your bacht/fate' = 'to your fortune') and functions as the beneficiary ('may it come to your fortune'); 'هرگونه کار' = 'in every kind of undertaking' (هرگونه = any kind; کار = deed/affair). Word order places the verb first (typical of classical poetic style) and the beneficiary and domain after it.
segments.s246-g261: 'نتابید' is the negative past of تابیدن in the sense 'to endure/submit' (here: 'did not endure/submit'). Word order is Verb–Subject–prepositional phrase: 'نتابید رستم ز فرمان تو' literally 'did not endure Rostam from/ because of your command' → 'Rostam did not submit to your command'. The preposition 'ز' marks cause/relation ('because of'/'from').
segments.s246-g262: 'دلش بسته' is a small NP: 'دل' (heart) + enclitic 'ش' = 'his heart' + predicate adjective 'بسته' (bound/tied/attached). 'دیدم' is the speaker-verb 'I saw' (introducing what was observed). 'بپیمان تو' is an archaic fused form meaning 'to/with your pact' (essentially 'bound to your covenant'); here that phrase completes what was seen. The phrase as a whole: 'I saw his heart bound to your pledge.'
segments.s246-g263: 'چو' = 'when' (temporal conjunction). 'آن نامه' = 'that letter'. 'شاه دادم' = 'I gave (it) to the king' (دادم = I gave; شاه here the indirect object). 'بدوی' is an archaic/dialectal enclitic or duplication meaning 'to him' / emphatic dative ('to him' = to the king); the form is irregular in the provided senses and functions as a dative clitic.
segments.s246-g264: 'بمالید' is a past / aorist verb 'he rubbed / smeared'. 'بر' is the preposition 'on/upon', governing the following noun 'نامه‌بر' (the letter‑carrier / messenger). 'چشم و روی' = 'eyes and face' (chained as two coordinated objects). The clause describes the king's action on the messenger: he rubbed (something) on the messenger's eyes and face.
segments.s246-g81: بدل گفت is an adverbial + verb phrase: بدل here functions like “in reply / in return,” followed by گفت (past ‘said’). کاری نو آمد بشاه is another clause with poetic word order: کاری نو (a new matter) + آمد (came; simple past) + بشاه (بِ شاه, ‘to the king’ contracted as is common in poetry). Together: “In reply he said, ‘a new matter has come to the king.’”
segments.s246-g82: فرستاده گیوست is a small nominal clause: فرستاده (the messenger) + گیوست, where -ست is the archaic/copular ending attached to the name (literally ‘is Giv’ → ‘the messenger is Giv’). کامد براه is an archaic verbal phrase: کامد (comes/arrives — an older present/past form) + براه (poetic contraction of به راه, ‘on the road / on the way’). Together: “a messenger of Giv is coming on the road.”
segments.s246-g83: چو is the subordinating conjunction ‘when’; نزدیک شد is a compound verbal idea meaning ‘became near’ → ‘approached’ (نزدیک + شد, with شد here as the past/copro form ‘became’); پهلوان سپاه places the subject (پهلوان) and its complement (سپاه, ‘the host/army’) in the poetic order ‘the champion of the host’ or more naturally ‘the champion approached the army.’
segments.s246-g84: نیایش کنان is an adverbial participial phrase: نیایش (prayer) + کنان (doing/so) → ‘praying/supplicating.’ برگرفتند is past plural ‘they took up / they took’ and is followed by its object راه (road/way). The normal prose order راه برگرفتند is inverted here (verb before object) for metrical/poetic effect: ‘they set out on the road, praying.’
segments.s246-g85: بپرسید is past ‘he asked’ (third-person). دستان here functions as the thing requested or the group addressed (ambiguous in the manuscript tradition — literally ‘tales/hands’), and ز ایرانیان uses the poetic preposition ز (= از) ‘from / concerning the Iranians.’ The clause thus means roughly ‘he asked (for news / addressed) the Iranians.’
segments.s246-g86: This is a coordinated prepositional phrase: ز شاه و ز پیکار تورانیان uses repeated ز (‘from/about’) for emphasis/poetry. شاه = ‘the king’; پیکار تورانیان = ‘the fight/battle (with) the Turanians’ (noun + genitive/attributive). So it reads ‘about the king and about the fighting with the Turanians.’
segments.s246-g87: درود بزرگان is a possessive phrase ‘the greeting of the elders’ (درود + بزرگان). بدستان here appears as an adjective/noun meaning ‘the unlucky / ill‑favored’ (poetic usage) and بداد is given in the text form but functions as the verb ‘gave / delivered’ in context (an older verbal form). Together: ‘He gave the elders’ greeting to the ill‑favored.’
segments.s246-g88: ز شاه و ز گردان فرخ نژاد repeats the preposition ز (‘from / by’) for two sources: شاه (‘the king’) and گردان فرخ نژاد (‘the troop/band of Farrokh‑nejad’ where فرخ نژاد is a compound epithet ‘fortunate/blessed lineage’). This marks the sources/agents associated with the greeting.
segments.s246-g1: نویسنده is the nominative subject ('the scribe/author'); نامه را shows the definite/accusative object marked by را; پیش خواند is a compound verbal expression meaning 'read aloud / recited before (someone)'. Word order is poetically inverted (verb follows object), but grammatically this is Subject + Object + Verb, with پیش specifying the manner/place (in the presence of / before someone).
segments.s246-g2: وزین modifies داستان ('this weighty / grave tale'); چند با is an adverbial phrase 'several / a few times' (چند + با); او is the third-person pronoun 'him'; براند is 3rd-person singular (poetic/historical present) of راند, here functioning as 'related / sent forth / told' — i.e., he repeatedly addressed/recited the tale to him. The clause therefore says he related that weighty story to him on several occasions.
segments.s246-g3: برستم here functions as a proper name (agent/recipient of the order) rather than the lexical verb 'برس'; یکی نامه is an indefinite object 'a certain letter'; فرمود is the past polite verb 'commanded/ordered'; شاه is the subject 'the king', placed after the verb due to poetic inversion (Verb + Subject order is common in classical verse).
segments.s246-g4: نوشتن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to write' (specifying the action required); ز marks origin/source 'from (or by)'; مهتر is the source/agent here 'the elder / chief'; سوی introduces the destination 'to / toward'; نیکخواه is the addressee 'the well-wisher / benevolent one'. Together this gives the directive: 'to be written by the chief to the well-wisher.'
segments.s246-g5: که‌ای is an archaic/exclamatory vocative particle (combining که + ای) addressing someone ('O you ...'); پهلوان زاده is a compound meaning 'born of/into a champion' or simply 'hero-born'; پر هنر is an adjectival phrase 'full (پر) of art/skill (هنر)'. The phrase is a vocative and descriptive epithet: 'O hero-born, full of skill.'
segments.s246-g6: ز is the preposition 'from / out of' (poetic); گردان لشکر is a unit phrase 'the troop / ranks of the army' (گردان modifying لشکر); برآورده is a past-participial/compound form of برآوردن here meaning 'having raised / brought up' and combines with سر ('head') to mean 'he lifted/raised his head' or figuratively 'he emerged/appeared (as leader)'. The segment describes emergence from the army ranks.
segments.s246-g7: دل is 'heart' used metaphorically for core/essence or beloved one; شهریاران is plural of شهریار 'princes / kings' (i.e., the nobility); و is the coordinating 'and'; پشت here means 'back / support' (metaphorical: the mainstay); کیان refers to the Kayanid dynasty or 'the kings'. The phrase pairs two metaphors: 'the heart of the princes and the support of the kings.'
segments.s246-g8: بفرمان is a prepositional phrase 'by the order / command of'; هر کس is 'every/any person' ('whoever'); کمر بر میان is a fused idiom: کمر 'waist / girdle' with بر 'on/upon' and میان 'middle' — literally 'girds his belt about his middle', i.e. 'who girds his loins' (prepares for action). Syntactically, the whole phrase qualifies the preceding description: 'at the command of anyone who girds his loins.'
segments.s246-g265: عنان (rein) is the concrete noun 'rein/bridle' followed by با + another عنان (instrument or thing paired with the first). من here marks possession ('my') attached after the second عنان. اندر is the archaic locative/prepositional adverb 'in/into/within' placed before the verb. ببست is a 3rd person singular past form (classical poetic form of بستن) meaning 'he bound/tied.' The whole phrase literally reads 'he bound the rein with my rein/onto my rein.'
segments.s246-g266: چنانچون is a classical comparative particle 'just as/just like.' بود is the past copula 'was' here linking to the following predicative phrase. گرد functions as a prepositional/adverbial 'around/about' (classical usage) and خسروپرست is a predicate noun 'one who worships the king' or 'sycophant.' Together: 'just as he was about/among the king‑worshipper.'
segments.s246-g267: برفتم is the 1st person singular past of رفتن in an older/poetic form meaning 'I went/advanced' (the provided lemma senses do not match, so the sense is marked -1). من is the explicit subject 'I.' از پیش is a classical adverbial phrase 'from the front/forward.' تا introduces a purpose clause ('so that/in order that'). با before تو شاه addresses the addressee ('to/with you, O king') with تو the vocative 'you' and شاه a title used vocatively.
segments.s246-g268: بگویم here is a subjunctive/intentional present ('that I may / I should tell'), functioning as the verb of the purpose clause introduced by تا; the provided lemma senses do not match the verb form so this sense is -1. که introduces the content clause 'that ...' and آمد is the past tense 'has come.' تهمتن is used as the proper epithet/name 'Tahmätan' (an epithet of Rostam), ز راه is the poetic prepositional phrase 'by/from the road.' Word order: clause introduced by که followed by the finite verb آمد and its complements.
segments.s246-g269: بگیو is an archaic imperative or hortative 'say!/tell!' used to introduce reported speech or an instruction. آنگهی is a temporal adverb 'then/thereupon.' گفت is the 3rd person past 'he said' but the provided lemma senses are for 'conversation' not the verb form, so its sense here is marked -1. رستم کجاست is the direct interrogative 'Where is Rostam?' with کجاست (کجا + ست) functioning as 'where is ...'.
segments.s246-g270: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for/that'). پشت here is used metaphorically as 'support/backing' (not just physical back), and بزرگی means 'greatness' — together 'the support of greatness.' و links the two appositive predicates. تخم is 'seed/offspring/origin' (here 'offspring/lineage'), and وفاست is a fused form meaning 'is of fidelity' or 'is fidelity' (وفا + است), i.e. 'is the offspring/support of loyalty.' Grammatically the clause predicates qualities of Rostam introduced by که.
segments.s246-g271: گرامیش کردن is a verbal noun (گرامیش 'honoring/respect' + کردن light verb) meaning 'to honor' or 'to show honor.' سزاوار is an adjective 'deserving/fit/appropriate,' and هست is the copula 'is.' Together: 'To honor (him) is appropriate.'
segments.s246-g272: که introduces the content clause giving the reason. نیکی نمایست is a classical verbal phrase: نیکی 'goodness/good deed' and نمایست (an archaic/poetic form of 'shows/does' or 'is manifested') — here meaning 'he does/shows goodness.' و links the next predicate خسروپرست, a predicate noun 'one who worships the king / a sycophant.' The clause gives the grounds for honoring him.
segments.s246-g145: همان ( demonstrative) = 'that/the same', placed before the noun to point back to something mentioned earlier. جام رخشنده is a noun phrase 'shining cup' (جام = cup, رخشنده = radiant/bright) with the adjective after the noun, a normal Persian order. بنهاد is the past of نهادن 'to put/place' (classical form) and so the whole segment means 'he placed that shining cup before (him)'. پیش here functions as a locative/adverb meaning 'before/in front (of)'.
segments.s246-g146: بهر سو نگه کرد is a short clause: بهر سو = 'toward every side' (بهر = toward/to, سو = side/direction). نگه = 'gaze/look' and کرد is the light perfective auxiliary 'did/made' combining with نگه to yield 'turned/kept his gaze' (i.e. 'looked'). ز اندازه بیش is an adverbial phrase meaning 'beyond measure/excessively' (ز = 'from/of' used poetically; اندازه = 'measure/extent'; بیش = 'excess/too much').
segments.s246-g147: This line uses inverted classical word order. بتوران is the indirect object/directional 'to Turan' placed up front. نشان داد is the verb phrase 'gave/sent a sign/token' (نشان = token/sign, داد = gave). ز + و should be read as ز او (classical contraction) meaning 'of it/from it' (زو here = 'from it' or 'of it'), and شهریار at the end is the subject 'the king' — typical epic inversion that places the verb before the overt subject.
segments.s246-g148: ببند is the verb 'bind/fasten' (shape identical to the present/imperfect imperative in New Persian; in epic narrative it functions as a simple past/action: 'he bound/fastened'). گران = 'heavy' modifies the (understood) object (e.g. belt). The conjunction و links to the next clause. ببد is a classical past form meaning 'became/was turned' (i.e. 'it became'), and روزگار is 'fate/time' — together ببد روزگار = 'and fortune/time grew (adverse)'.
segments.s246-g149: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. در جام = 'in the cup' (در = in, جام = cup). کیخسرو is the proper name 'Ki-Khosrow'. ایدون نمود is an archaic literary verb cluster: ایدون (a poetic/archaic form of 'come/appear') plus نمود (past of نمودن 'to show/appear'), together meaning 'appeared' or 'was revealed'.
segments.s246-g150: سوی پهلوانم is a directional phrase 'toward my champion' (سوی = toward, پهلوانم = 'my hero/champion' with the first‑person possessive clitic). دوانید is the past verb 'ran' (دوانیدن = to run; classical past form used here), and زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying the action. Word order places the direction phrase before the verb, a normal option in Persian.
segments.s246-g151: کنون آمدم = 'now I came' (کنون = now; آمدم = 1st‑person past 'I came'). با دلی پر امید is a prepositional phrase 'with a heart full of hope' (با = with; دلی = 'a heart' (دِل + ezāfe implied), پر = 'full (of)'; امید = 'hope'). The adjective 'پر' precedes the noun 'امید' in the usual Persian 'full of X' construction.
segments.s246-g152: This is a simple descriptive coordinate phrase. دو رخساره = 'two cheeks/faces' (دو = two; رخساره = face/cheek) with the adjective زرد 'pale/wan' following the noun. و links the two coordinated noun phrases. دو دیده سپید = 'two (white) eyes' (دیده = eye; سپید = white/bright). Adjectives appear after the nouns, as is common in Persian.
segments.s246-g113: ز اسب اندر آمد is a poetic way to say 'he came off the horse' (ز = 'from', اسب = 'horse', اندر آمد = 'came in/entered' but here 'came down / dismounted' by idiom). گرفتش is the past verb گرفت + enclitic direct object -ش ('seized him'). In the line the subject ببر ('tiger') follows the verb (postposed subject), a common poetic inversion: 'گرفتش ببر' = 'the tiger seized him.'
segments.s246-g114: بپرسیدش is past/actional 'he/they questioned him' (پرسید with the prefixed ب- used in Classical Persian forms and -ش = 'him'). از introduces the person or topic of the questioning ('about / concerning'), so از خسرو تاجور = 'about Khosrow the trader/merchant.' Word order: verb + object-pronoun followed by an از-phrase marking the thing asked about.
segments.s246-g115: This is an enumerative list introduced by repeated ز ('from'). Here وز is a fused poetic sequence functioning like و + ز ('and from') linking names: 'ز گودرز وز طوس وز گستهم' = 'from Goudarz and from Tus and from Gostaham.' گستهم appears in form identical to a verb but here functions as a proper name in the list (poetry often preserves archaic forms).
segments.s246-g116: ز گردان لشکر is 'from the troop/division of the army' — گردان + لشکر form a compound (troop of the army). همه بیش و کم is an idiomatic phrase 'everyone, big and small' (همه = all; بیش و کم = more and less / greater and lesser), describing that the whole host, in every rank, is implied. Note poetic ordering omits connective particles that prose might supply.
segments.s246-g117: Again an enumerative string with initial ز = 'from'. The plain و between names is the normal 'and'; وز here functions as the fused 'and from' (و + ز). The sequence lists proper names (شاپور, فرهاد, بیژنا) — note that بیژنا is an obscure/possibly dialectal name form preserved in the poem.
segments.s246-g118: Same pattern: ز introduces the group 'from Roham and Gorgin' (رهام و گرگین). وز again links to the final item; هرتنا is an obscure word/name here (the lexical list gives uncertain senses), so in this line it should be read as a final item in the roster. The passage is a catalogue of warriors.
segments.s246-g119: چو is the conjunction 'when'. آواز بیژن is 'the voice of Bijan' (noun + name in genitive relation). رسیدش is رسید + the enclitic -ش; here it means 'reached him' (the sound reached his ear). بگوش is the locative 'to the ear' (ب- + گوش), so the whole phrase = 'when Bijan's voice reached his ear.'
segments.s246-g120: برآمد is 'arose/issued forth' (past). بناکام is an adverb/adj meaning 'unsuccessful / in vain' — here modifying the cry. ازو is the fused form of از او 'from him' (classical enclitic-style). یک خروش = 'a single cry' (یک = one; خروش = cry/roar). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect (verb + modifier + از-phrase + noun).
segments.s246-g9: This clause names the addressee and their relation to the speaker: the (pro)noun phrase is fronted, then a genitive phrase marks source. The sequence 'توی / از نیاکان' places the person in relation to the speaker's ancestors (an archaic poetic ordering). 'مرا' is the object/beneficiary (to me) and 'یادگار' is a nominal predicate meaning 'remembrance/memento'. No finite verb appears; Persian often uses such nominal predication in poetry (the copula is implied).
segments.s246-g10: 'همیشه' is an adverb modifying the following participial adjective phrase. 'کمربسته' is a compound participle/adjective (literally 'belt‑fastened') meaning 'girded/armed' and functions predicatively with 'یادگار' from the previous segment. 'کارزار' is the noun governed by 'کمربسته' (girded for battle); the adjective follows its noun in this poetic word order, emphasizing readiness for combat.
segments.s246-g11: Linear structure: indirect object 'ترا' (to you) is marked by the enclitic + object marker, then the verb 'داد' (gave) and its agent/subject 'گردون' (the sky/heaven/fate). 'بمردی' is an adverbial phrase ('in manhood' / 'with manly valour') modifying the giving; 'پلنگ' is the tangible gift/metaphorical epithet (leopard/panther). Note the poetic use of animal epithets as bestowed virtues.
segments.s246-g12: 'بدریا' is a locative phrase ('in/into the sea') setting the domain for the next image. 'ز' introduces cause or source ('because of' / 'from'), followed by 'بیمت' ('your fear' / 'your dread' — here used as the cause affecting the sea). 'خروشان' is an adjective 'roaring/tumultuous' modifying 'نهنگ' (whale). The verbless line links back to 'داد' (gave) by parataxis: different domains (land/sea) receive mighty creatures/images.
segments.s246-g13: The direct object 'جهان را' (the world) is fronted and marked with the object marker 'را'. 'ز دیوان مازندران' is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'from the (diwan of) Mazandaran' giving the source/thing removed — in epic usage 'دیوان مازندران' refers to the hostile beings of Mazandaran. The word order emphasizes the object affected ('the world').
segments.s246-g14: 'بشستی' here is a past / perfective form addressed to second person (poetic past), carrying the action against the preceding object (you washed/cleared). The conjunction 'و' links the two past actions. The following cluster 'کندی بدان را سران' contains a verb ('کندی' — here a past tense verb 'you uprooted' / 'you plucked away' in context), the directional/prefixed element 'ب' (be-/to), the infinitive/root 'دان' (used in composite verbal morphology), the object marker 'را', and 'سران' (the leaders/chiefs). Together they mean you removed/uprooted their leaders from their place; the order is typical for Persian poetic syntax where objects and particles can be separated.
segments.s246-g15: 'چه مایه' is an exclamatory quantifier ('what a quantity / how many') modifying the noun phrase that follows. 'سر تاجداران' is a genitive construction 'the heads of the crown‑bearers' (i.e. crowned sovereigns). 'ز گاه' is a temporal/occasional phrase 'at times' or 'from occasion to occasion' and sets the time-frame for the following actions. The phrase is rhetorical/exclamatory and prepares the listener for a sequence of feats.
segments.s246-g16: Two past verbs in coordination: 'ربودی' (you seized/dragged off) and 'برکندی' (you uprooted/dislodged). The conjunction 'و' links them; 'از پیشگاه' is the prepositional phrase 'from the presence' (i.e. from before the royal court/throne) specifying the source from which those heads were taken. Both verbs are second‑person past, forming a punchy, cataloguing statement of violent deeds.
segments.s246-g273: بفرمود is a past, honorific/polite verb meaning 'he commanded/said' (verb-first order is common in poetic prose). خسرو is the agent (the king) following the verb; here it denotes the sovereign. بفرزانگان is the indirect object introduced by the prefixed b- (به) meaning 'to/for the sages' — the group being addressed.
segments.s246-g274: This phrase is a continuation of the addressees: the prefixed ب- again functions like به 'to'; مهتر (here merged as بمهتر) signals 'chief/elder' and governs the following nouns in apposition. نژادان و مردانگان list the groups 'nobles/by lineage' and 'men/warriors' respectively; و is the coordinating 'and'. (The form بمهتر is a poetic/contracted presentation of به مهتر.)
segments.s246-g275: پذیره شدن combines the noun پذیره 'reception/acceptance' with the light verb شدن to form a passive/verbal nominal phrase 'to be received' or 'a reception to take place'. پیش او 'in his presence/before him' specifies the locus, and با سپاه 'with an army' indicates accompaniment or means.
segments.s246-g276: که introduces a relative clause ('which/who/that') modifying the preceding clause (the army or those received). آمد is simple past 'came'. بفرمان خسرو is an agent/means phrase 'by the order of Khosrow' and براه is an adverbial 'on the road / en route', placed after the verb in the poetic word order.
segments.s246-g277: بگفتند is past plural 'they said' often used to mean 'they declared/named'. The following proper names are the direct objects; را after کشواد marks that name as a definite/direct object (definiteness/topic marking). Word order places verb before its object(s) in this poetic register.
segments.s246-g278: This continues the object-list: شه (شاه) functions as 'king' and combines with نوذران ('of/related to Nowzar') to form 'the Nowzran king' (a titled noun phrase). طوس and فرهاد are additional names joined by و 'and'. The final را marks the whole list as a definite object.
segments.s246-g279: دو بهره is a quantified noun phrase 'two shares/contingents'. ز is the poetic form of از 'from', introducing the source: گردان 'troop/company' qualified by گردنکشان 'those that lift/strain their necks' — an epithet meaning 'proud ones' or 'rebels'. The genitive relationship is implicit: 'two shares from the troop of the proud.'
segments.s246-g280: چه here functions to link/add ('and also' / 'likewise') introducing another source: از 'from'. گرزداران is a plural attribute 'mace‑bearers' (those who carry clubs/maces). مردمکشان is مردمک + شان (their ...) and here refers to small persons/young men belonging to them; the suffix -شان marks possession ('their ...').
segments.s246-g89: همه درد دل پیش دستان بخواند is a compact clause meaning “he recited/uttered all the heart’s pains before Dastān.” همه (precedes the noun phrase) quantifies درد دل (‘all the pains of the heart’). پیش is a preposition meaning “in the presence of / before,” and بخواند is an old narrative/epic perfective/past infinitive-type form meaning “he recited” or “he poured out (in speech)”; word order is topic (whole NP) → locative (پیش …) → verb, typical for Persian narrative style.
segments.s246-g90: This short clause follows as explanation/complaint: غم پور گم بوده با او براند. پور is used as ‘son’/‘youth’ (a descriptive epithet here: “the sorrow‑stricken son”), گم بوده is an archaic/perfective sequence “had been lost” (بودِه = been/was), با او records accompaniment or association “with him,” and براند is a present/habitual or narrative verb form of راندن (here ‘went away/was gone’ or ‘was driven off’); the clause therefore reports that the sorrowful youth was lost/absent and went off with him. Note: the phrase is somewhat compressed/poetic and the exact referent of پور is epistolary/epic‑style (“the young man, son”) rather than a literal patronymic.
segments.s246-g91: همی گفت رویم نبینی برنگ opens with the frequent epic particle همی, here marking continuous or iterative action (“he kept saying” or “he then said”). گفت is the narrative past ‘said.’ رویم is a possession+nominalized face form (‘my face’/‘my appearance’) and نبینی is negative subordinate (2nd person singular subjunctive/imperative sense, “you will not see”). برنگ (literally ‘of color, hue’) here refers to a change or particular color/complexion; in context the clause says “you will not see my face of that color” — i.e. ‘you won’t see my usual color/complexion.’ Word order: particle → verb → object (possessed noun) → negative verb phrase → predicate noun.
segments.s246-g92: ز خون مژه پشت پایم بلنگ is a terse, poetic descriptive clause introduced by the poetic preposition ز ‘from/because of.’ خون مژه is a genitive construction ‘the blood of the eyelash’ (a conventional image in Persian poetry), پشتِ پایم is a possessed locative ‘the back of my foot / my heel,’ and بلنگ is an obscure or dialectal poetic adjective/noun here describing the afflicted condition of that part (the exact nuance is archaic/obscure in classical diction). Grammatically, this gives the reason/result: “because of the blood of (my) eyelash, the back of my foot is (in the state called) بلنگ.”
segments.s246-g93: ازان پس نشان تهمتن بخواست is a short narrative clause: ازان پس ‘after that,’ نشان ‘a sign/token’ is the object, تهمتن is the addressee/name (Tahmātan, an epithet), and بخواست is the past form of خواستن used here to mean ‘he asked/desired.’ Word order is temporal adverbial → object → indirect object/name → verb, typical of Persian narrative compression.
segments.s246-g94: بپرسید و گفتش که رستم کجاست contains two coordinated actions: بپرسید (past narrative form ‘he asked/inquired’) + و ‘and’ + گفتش (‘he said to him’ — the object clitic attached to گفت indicates ‘to him’). که introduces the content clause (‘that’/‘where’) and Rِستم کجاست is the embedded interrogative clause ‘where is Rostam?’; ست is the copula (is). The verb forms are narrative past used in epic recounting.
segments.s246-g95: بدو گفت رستم بنخچیر گور is an addressed report: بدو ‘to him’ (indirect object), گفت ‘he said’ (narrative past), then a clause naming the subject and its complement: رستم (Rostam) followed by بنخچیر گور — a genitive/complement phrase meaning ‘with/by the spoil/prey of the onager’ (بنخچیر = booty/spoil; گور in epic context can mean a wild ass/onager or be part of a compound). The sequence places the indirect object before the speech report (a standard clitic/word‑order pattern) and then reports the content.
segments.s246-g96: بیاید همانا که برگشت هور is the speech content: بیاید is a subjunctive/optative or narrative-future form ‘will come’ or ‘may come,’ همانا is a particle ‘verily/indeed,’ که introduces the reason/clause and برگشت هور means ‘Hūr has returned’ (برگشت as ‘return’ and هور here a proper name or epithet). Together: “He will come; indeed, Hūr has returned.” The verbs are in the narrative/epic mode and همانا gives emphatic confirmation.
segments.s246-g201: The temporal phrase 'سه روز' ('three days') sets the duration. 'اندرین خان من' is an older word order: 'اندرین' ('in this/inside this') + 'خان من' ('my khan/house/inn') — here functioning as a locative/possessive phrase. 'شاد باش' is an imperative (second person singular) telling the addressee to 'be merry'.
segments.s246-g202: 'ز' is the archaic/prepositional particle 'from' (here 'free from'). The coordinated phrase 'رنج و ز اندیشه' ('suffering and from thought/worry') lists what the addressee should be free of. 'آزاد باش' repeats the imperative 'be free' (second person singular).
segments.s246-g203: 'که' here introduces an explanatory clause ('for/because'). 'این خانه' is the grammatical subject ('this house'). 'زان خانه' ('from that house') indicates source or comparison, and 'بخشیده نیست' is a passive/perfect sense 'is not given/granted' (negative copula), so the clause explains that this house is not granted by/deriving from that other house.
segments.s246-g204: This clause means roughly 'for me, with you, treasure, body and soul are one.' The token 'م' + 'را' in the list corresponds to the dative/object 'مرا' ('to/for me') split here; 'با تو' ('with you') marks accompaniment; 'گنج و تن و جان' is a coordinated noun list; the sentence ends with the copula 'یکیست' ('is one'), a standard Persian predicate-final verb expressing identity/unity.
segments.s246-g205: The omitted/preceding numeral 'چهارم' ('the fourth') supplies the temporal frame ('on the fourth [day]'). 'سوی شهر ایران' is a directional prepositional phrase 'toward the city of Iran.' 'شویم' is first-person plural present/subjunctive/hortative from 'شدن' used here as 'let us go / we shall go' — a common classical Persian way to express 'we will go' or 'let us go'.
segments.s246-g206: 'بنزدیک' is an adverb/prepositional adverb meaning 'near' or 'close at hand.' 'شاه دلیران' is a noun phrase where 'دلیران' ('the brave') qualifies 'شاه' ('the king') in an attributive/genitive sense. The clause closes with 'شویم' again ('let us come/let us draw near'), so the sense is 'let us approach the king of the brave.'
segments.s246-g207: 'چو' is the temporal conjunction 'when.' 'رستم' is the subject name and 'چنین گفت' = 'said thus/so' (past 3rd singular). 'بر جست' is a compound verbal phrase: 'بر' + 'جست' meaning 'leapt up' or 'sprang forward.' The following name 'گیو' is the actor who makes that motion. Note: the supplied senses list for 'گفت' in the data does not include the straightforward 'said' sense, so that specific sense is not matched in the given options.
segments.s246-g208: 'ببوسید' is the past (3rd person) form 'he kissed' (literary). It is followed by the coordinated direct objects 'دست و سر و پای' ('hand and head and foot'). The final token 'نیو' is the intended target/name whose limbs are kissed; however, the provided sense options for 'نیو' do not match a person-name here, so that sense is not available among the supplied choices.
segments.s246-g121: This unit contains the speaker and the formulaic 'thus said' plus a vocative. برستم functions as a nominative (here a personal name or agent) before the speech formula چنین گفت (چنین = 'thus', گفت = 'said' in the simple past). کای بآفرین is a vocative/title: کای is the Kayanian title 'Kay' (used before a royal name) and بآفرین is an epithet/name attached to it; together they address the king directly.
segments.s246-g122: This is an imperative clause: گزین is the imperative 'choose' (verb first, typical in older/poetic Persian), followed by its object همه خسروان زمین 'all the kings of the land' (همه + plural noun + genitive/locative زمین). The object phrase is post-verbal, with زمین specifying the domain ('of the earth/land').
segments.s246-g123: چنان شاد گشتم is a predicate: چنان/چنین 'so/thus' + شاد 'happy' with گشتم (1st‑person past of گشتن) 'I became'. بدیدار تو is a prepositional phrase 'at seeing you' (بدیدار = به دیدار in poetic spelling, 'at/for the meeting of', followed by the pronoun تو 'you'), giving the cause/context for the happiness.
segments.s246-g124: بدین پرسش خوب و گفتار تو = 'by/for this good question and your speech.' بدین is the poetic contraction of به + این ('by/with this'); پرسش is the noun 'question'; خوب qualifies پرسش; و connects گفتار 'speech' and تو is the possessor 'your' placed after the noun (normal in Persian poetry).
segments.s246-g125: درستند is the predicate 'they are right/true' (third person plural). The following clause ازین هرک بردی تو نام is a dependent relative clause: ازین = 'from these', هرک = 'whichever/each one', بردی (2nd person past) = 'you took/won', and نام is the object meaning 'name' or 'reputation' (here 'the name/fame you acquired'). The explicit pronoun تو emphasizes the 2nd person subject.
segments.s246-g126: ازیشان = از + ایشان 'from them' (source), فراوان modifies the following nouns ('many' / 'abundant'), and درود و پیام = 'greetings and messages.' Together this says that from those people there come plentiful greetings and messages.
segments.s246-g127: نبینی is a negative interrogative 'do you not see' (2nd person present/imperative‑question). That is followed by a content clause introduced by که 'that/what'. بر من بپیران سرم is an older/poetic order meaning roughly 'upon me, in front of the elders, my head' — بپیران = به پیران ('before/to the elders'), and سرم is 'my head' (possessed by the enclitic -م). The phrase asks the addressee whether they see what has happened to the speaker in the sight of the elders.
segments.s246-g128: This clause spells out the misfortune: چه آمد 'what came / what has happened' + ز بخت بد 'from ill fortune' + اندر خورم 'upon me' using خوردن in the sense 'to befall.' آمد is the simple past 'came/occurred' and خورم is 1st person singular meaning 'befell me' in classical usage.
segments.s246-g217: بسالار خوان is a compound title 'master/chief of the table/banquet' (noun + noun). گفت is the simple past 3rd-person singular 'said' introducing a command. پیش آر خوان is an imperative clause: پیش 'forward/in front', آر is an archaic/poetic imperative 'bring' (literally 'cause to come'), and خوان here means 'reciter/performer' — together: 'bring the reciter forward.'
segments.s246-g218: This is an imperative clause with the direct object before the verb, typical in Persian commands. بزرگان و فرزانگان is a coordinated definite object marked by را (object marker); بخوان is the imperative/optative form (second-person/polite plural) here meaning 'call/summon' or 'read/recite' depending on context — syntactically it is the verb taking the preceding marked noun phrase as its object.
segments.s246-g219: This is an adverbial-place + list of personal names: زوَاره gives the location ('at Zowārah'), followed by a coordination of proper names joined by و. The sequence is simply a nominative list of who acted (they).
segments.s246-g220: نشستند is past plural 'they sat down' (predicate). بر is the preposition 'on/at/by' marking the location; خوان‌سالار is a compound noun 'master of the table/banquet-master'. نیو here is a noun 'ney' (reed‑flute) that functions as accompanying information (instrument present at the scene). Word order: subject/place — predicate — locative phrase.
segments.s246-g221: This clause has two coordinated past verbs. بخوردند is past 3rd‑person plural; in banquet contexts it means 'they ate'. خوان is the object 'the banquet/table'. بپرداختند is another past 3rd‑person plural verb meaning here 'they attended to/settled (matters)' or 'they paid' — it follows the conjunction و linking the two actions.
segments.s246-g222: نشستنگه is a noun 'place/seat; assembly' (an area for sitting). رود here is the musical rūd (string instrument), not a river; و links the coordinated actions; می‌ساختند is the imperfective past 'they were making/they made' and in musical context means 'they made (music)/they composed or played' the instrument mentioned — so together: they set the seating and made/play the rūd music.
segments.s246-g223: نوازنده رود is an agent‑noun + object: 'player/performer of the rūd' (instrument). با is the preposition 'with', and میگسار is the companion 'cupbearer/wine‑companion' — the phrase means the musician arrived accompanied by the cupbearer.
segments.s246-g224: بیامد is simple past 'came'. بایوان is an archaic/uncertain form (likely 'to the iwan/portico' or similar locative), so its exact grammatical role is locative/adverbial. گوهر نگار is a compound epithet (literally 'jewel‑painted' or 'jewel‑adorned') used here as a descriptive or a proper epithet for a person or thing that arrives.
segments.s246-g49: چو introduces a temporal/conditional subordinate clause: 'when/if'. The phrase این نامه من is the object (this letter of mine) placed before the verb (typical Persian word order). بخوانی is 2nd-person singular present/subjunctive (here 'you read'); مپای is an archaic/discouraging particle meaning roughly 'do not delay / do not tarry', placed after the clause as an admonition.
segments.s246-g50: بزودی is an adverb ('soon/quickly') modifying the following imperatives. تو is the addressed pronoun (emphatic here). با + proper name (گیو) marks accompaniment. خیز is an archaic 2nd-person singular imperative ('rise!' from برخاستن). اندرآی is another imperative/jussive ('enter / come in'), creating a sequence of commands 'rise and enter'.
segments.s246-g51: بدان is an imperative formed from ب- + دان (literally 'know'); it tells the addressee to 'know/consider' what follows. تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that / in order that'). بدین کار (= به + این + کار) = 'in this matter' (prepositional phrase). با ما = 'with us'. بهم here attaches to the following verb (بهم زنی) and means 'into disorder / together' (i.e. the sense that produces disruption or mixing).
segments.s246-g52: زنی is here the verbal element combining with the previous بهم to form بهم زنی ('to upset/confound/overturn' or 'to interfere with/alter'); in classical Persian poetry the auxiliary ب- is often elided or split. رای فرخ = 'Farrokh's counsel/opinion' (رای = counsel). بهر بیش و کم literally 'for more and less' — i.e. 'to increase or decrease / to alter up or down' — فرخ's advice is to be adjusted.
segments.s246-g53: The repeated short prepositional/poetic particles (ز / وز) mean 'from' or are the poetic combination و + ز = 'and from' (listing sources): 'from men and from treasure and from desired things.' This is a coordinated list of what the speaker will provide.
segments.s246-g54: بیارم is a 1st-person singular verbal form 'I will bring / I bring' (classical/fixed-form future/intent). بپیش تو = 'before you / in your presence' (بپیش = 'before, toward; in front of'). آراسته is a past participle/adjective 'adorned/arrayed' describing how what is brought will be presented.
