segments.s246-g55: بفرخ is an optative/jussive form meaning 'may it prosper' or 'may it be fortunate' (a wish). پی و بر شده together modify نام تو: شده is the past participle of شدن ('become'), and پی و بر act as adverbial/qualifying elements describing how the name is (here roughly 'continuously / broadly / exaltedly'); the whole phrase attaches to نام تو ('your name')—literary word order puts the verb/wish before the nominal phrase it governs.
segments.s246-g56: This is a mirroring optative clause: ز توران = 'from Turan'; برآید is 3rd-person singular optative/subjunctive ('may ... come / may ... arise'); همه کام تو = 'all your wishes' (همه = all; کام = wish/desire; تو = your). Literal order is 'From Turan may arise all your desires' — i.e. 'may all your wishes be fulfilled from Turan.'
segments.s246-g129: چه here is an exclamative 'what/how' introducing astonishment; چشم بد is a noun phrase 'evil eye' (gaze) and آمد is a simple past 'came/befell'. بگودرزیان names the affected party (a family) in the oblique/dative sense (the one the misfortune fell upon) with the preposition omitted, a common classical structure.
segments.s246-g130: کزان ('from which') links this clause back to the 'evil eye' just mentioned. سود ما را is 'our profit' with را marking the definite object/thing affected; سر آمد is a small idiomatic sequence (سر + آمد) meaning 'came to be / turned into'; زیان is the outcome 'loss'. The verb آمد is past, so the whole clause states a resulting change.
segments.s246-g131: ز گیتی ('in/ of the world') gives the setting. مرا here functions as a dative/possessor ('to me' / 'for me'); in the manuscript the elements are split (m + را), with را after the pronoun as an older order. خود is an emphatic 'my own', یکی پور بود is an existential past 'there was one son'. بود is the past copula/existential verb.
segments.s246-g132: همم پور is a poetic collocation: همم (literally 'aspiration/hope') used here as 'the object of my hopes' or 'my pride', followed by پور 'son'. و هم introduces the second coordinated description 'and also'; پاک دستور بود predicates that he was 'of pure/orderly (noble) lineage' with بود as past copula. The sequence gives two qualities of the son.
segments.s246-g133: شد is the past/resultative 'became' or 'happened'; از چشم من literally 'from my eye' = 'from my sight' (a fixed idiom). در جهان 'in the world' is a locative adjunct, and ناپدید is the predicate 'vanished/was unseen'. The verb-first placement (شد ...) is typical of classical narrative compression.
segments.s246-g134: بدین (here written as ب + دین) means 'by/for/with this' or 'to this' and introduces the phrase 'بدین دودمان' = 'in/for this lineage'. کس چنین غم ندید is a negative past statement: کس ('anyone', used here as 'no one' in effect) + چنین 'such' + غم 'sorrow' + ندید 'saw not / has not seen' (classical negative past of دیدن). The clause asserts uniqueness of the grief.
segments.s246-g135: چنینم is 'I am thus/so' (a state statement). که introduces a subordinate/purpose/result clause 'that/so that'; بینی is the 2nd-person present subjunctive/indicative 'you see' (classical register can mean 'you shall see' or 'that you may see'). بپشت is a contracted classical prepositional phrase equivalent to برِ پشت 'on the back of', and ستور is 'steeds/horse(s)' — together: 'on the backs of steeds.'
segments.s246-g136: شب و روز 'night and day' is an adverbial phrase of time. تازان is an active participial/agentive plural meaning 'riders/chargers' or 'those who rush', and بتاریک is a prepositional/ directional form 'into darkness' (ب + تاریک). هور is a poetic noun (here 'Hūr' / 'light/splendour' or a place-name in context); together the phrase describes continual riding into the dark.
segments.s246-g137: ز introduces the reason/cause here ('because of Bijan'). شب و روز is an adverbial phrase 'night and day' (intensive, placed before the verb). چون + بیهشان is an archaic simile: چون 'as/like' and بیهشان (بیه + ـشان) functions as 'without him / bereft of him' (poetic/archaic), so the whole chunk reads 'because of Bijan, night and day, as if bereft of him.'
segments.s246-g138: بجستم is a past-form poetic verb meaning 'I sought/searched' (first person past). بهر سو is a directional phrase 'toward every side' (بهر = toward/for, سو = side/direction). ز هر کس نشان is the object phrase 'a sign from every person' (ز = from, هر کس = every person, نشان = sign/trace). Word order places the verb before the directional and source phrases, typical in poetic diction.
segments.s246-g139: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now.' شاه is the subject 'the king.' با marks accompaniment 'with' and is followed by the noun phrase جام گیتی‌نمای: جام 'cup' modified by the compound گیتی‌نمای ('giti‑نما' = world‑showing / world‑revealing), so 'the king with the world‑revealing cup.'
segments.s246-g140: بپیش here means 'in the presence of / before' (location). جهان‌آفرین is the title 'World‑Creator' (i.e. the Creator). شد is the past auxiliary/lexical 'became / was' and combines with the following predicate; بپای is an archaic/stative predicate meaning 'stood firm' or 'took his stand.' Together: 'he stood before the Creator.'
segments.s246-g141: چه مایه is an exclamatory intensifier 'what a magnitude of / what a ...'. خروشید (here a noun or verbalized noun) denotes the outcry/tumult. و کرد آفرین is a two‑part clause: کرد 'they made/did' and آفرین 'acclamation' (i.e. they cried 'bravo'). So the phrase means 'what an uproar there was, and they shouted approval.'
segments.s246-g142: بجشن is a prepositional phrase 'at the feast / at the festival.' کیان is the genitive/attributive 'of the Kayanids / the kings' (i.e. the royal festival). هرمز فرودین are names/titles in apposition (here read as 'Hormoz' (Ahura Mazda/Hormuzd) and 'Farvardin' (the month or name)), indicating which festival/time is meant: 'at the Kayanian feast of Hormoz in Farvardin.'
segments.s246-g143: پس آمد is 'then he came' (پس = then/after, آمد = came). ز آتشکده is 'from the fire‑temple' (ز = from). تا functions as 'until / at (the appointed) time' and بگاه means 'the hour / the time (dawn/appointed moment)'; together تا بگاه indicates he came at the appointed time or at dawn.
segments.s246-g144: کمربست is a past participial/past action 'he girded (his belt) / prepared himself.' و is the coordinating 'and.' بنهاد is an archaic past verb 'he placed/put' (here 'put on'), followed by the prepositional phrase بر سر کلاه 'a cap on his head' (بر = on, سر = head, کلاه = hat/cap). The sequence describes dressing: he girded himself and set a cap upon his head.
segments.s246-g17: بسا (an introductory quantifier) + noun phrase + relative clause: بسا 'many' quantifies دشمنان 'enemies'. کز is the older/poetic form of که از or که + ز, here a relative 'who/that (by)' introducing the agent phrase کز تو 'by you'. بیجان is a predicate adjective 'lifeless, dead', and شدست is the archaic fused past/perfect (شد + است → شده‌است) meaning 'has become / has been'. Word order is typical older Persian: quantifier → noun → relative clause with predicate at the end.
segments.s246-g18: Another parallel clause using the same pattern: بسا 'many' + coordinated noun phrase بوم و بر 'homeland(s) and region(s)' (a fixed collocation meaning land/places). کز تو introduces the agent 'by you', ویران is the predicate adjective 'ruined/desolate', and شدست (شد + است) is the perfective/predicative 'has become / has been'. The clause ends with the predicate as is customary in classical Persian.
segments.s246-g19: A nominal compound describing status: سر here means 'chief/leading position' (not anatomical head) and governs the abstract noun پهلوانی 'heroism/chivalry'. و links the second nominal 'لشکر پناه' where لشکر 'army' is governed by پناه 'refuge/protection' (i.e. 'the army's refuge'). This is a compact appositional phrase listing honors/roles.
segments.s246-g20: Location + possessor phrase: بنزدیک 'near, close at hand' gives the locative. شاهان 'the kings' names the landmark. ت is the second-person enclitic (here forming ترا = 'your') attached to the following noun; را is the emphatic/definite marker following that enclitic in this construction, and دستگاه 'court/establishment' is the possessed noun 'your court.' The literal order places the location and landmark before the possessed noun (typical in poetry): 'near the kings is your court.'
segments.s246-g21: همه 'all' quantifies جادوان 'the magicians/sorcerers' which is marked by the object/definite marker را. ببستی is the past tense (here second person) of بستن 'to bind/close' (classical preverbial 'ب' on past). بگرز is a verb meaning 'to turn/return' used here as a following verb that describes the result or consequence (literally 'you bound all the sorcerers, (so) they turned back').
segments.s246-g22: A short clause: بیفروختی is the poetic negative/perfect form (ب + فروختی) here meaning 'you did not sell' (or 'you have not sold'). تاج شاهان is a genitive/construct: 'the crown of the kings'. ببرز is a final word whose basic sense is 'to rise/appear' or can be a name/locative in different contexts; syntactically it stands after the object and can be read as an adverbial or an added note — here the clause plainly asserts 'you did not sell the kings' crown' with ببرز attached in the poem as a terse modifier.
segments.s246-g23: A correlative construction using چه ... و چه ... = 'whether ... or ... / be it ... or ...': the first چه introduces افراسیاب (the proper name) and the second چه introduces شاهان چین 'the kings of China.' This pattern lists alternative or exemplary powerful figures and uses چه in its emphatic/indefinite sense.
segments.s246-g24: نوشته here functions as 'written' (a past participle/nominal predicate). The phrase is inverted in poetic order: همه نام تو 'all your name' (i.e. your whole name/reputation) is the theme, and بر نگین 'upon the gem/signet' is the locative complement. So the clause reads: 'all your name is written upon the signet.'
segments.s246-g105: پذیره شدش is a passive-like expression: پذیره (reception/acceptance) plus شد with enclitic -ش (= 'him') yields 'he was received/accepted.' گیو names the person who was received. کامد is an archaic past-form 'came' and فراز is an adverbial 'up/forward/on high,' so کامد فراز = 'came forward/approached.' The clause orders these actions compactly in poetic inversion.
segments.s246-g106: پیاده شد is an idiom where پیاده ('on foot') + شد ('became') yields 'dismounted' (literally 'became on-foot'). از اسب marks the source ('from the horse'). و simply connects the next action. بردش is the past of بردن with enclitic -ش ('him') and here means 'took/led him,' though the provided sense list doesn't include this verbal sense. نماز is the object 'prayer(s),' so بردش نماز = 'led him to the prayers.'
segments.s246-g107: This is a compact, poetic adjective phrase with inversion: پر از آرزو دل literally places the predicate 'full of longing' before دل ('the heart'), i.e. 'the heart full of longing.' The repeated پر از marks two parallel descriptions (دل پر از آرزو, روی پر از رنگ). رنگ here means 'complexion/appearance,' so 'روی پر از رنگ' = 'his face flushed/colored.'
segments.s246-g108: به رخ برنهاد is a verbal phrase: به (to/toward) + رخ (face) + برنهاد (past of برنهاد, 'placed/raised up'), so 'he raised/turned his face.' از دو دیده دو جوی is an ablative/instrumental pattern 'from two eyes, two streams' (جوی = 'stream'), a terse way to say two streams (tears) flowed from his two eyes.
segments.s246-g109: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' رستم is the subject. دل گیو را uses the postposed possessor pattern with را as the definite/object marker: 'Giv's heart' (lit. 'the heart of Giv' marked as definite/object). خسته is a predicate adjective 'weary,' and دید is the reporting/perception verb 'saw' (i.e. 'saw that ... was weary').
segments.s246-g110: بآب مژه is an instrumental/ablative phrase 'with the water of the eyelash' — a poetic way to mean 'with tears.' روی او شسته is a perfect/predicative construction: شسته (washed) describes the state of his face (روی او). The final دید repeats the perception verb 'he saw (that ...).' Note the past participle شسته used predicatively.
segments.s246-g111: بدو گفت is 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto it'); گفت should be read here as the verb 'said' though the supplied senses don't list that verbal sense. باری is an exclamatory/discourse particle here (an interjection like 'alas' or 'indeed'), and تباهست is a stative verb 'is ruined' (تباه + است contracted), with کار 'the affair' as its subject/particle, so 'باری تباهست کار' = 'Alas/indeed, the affair is ruined.'
segments.s246-g112: بایوان is here a proper name (the supplied senses do not reflect that), followed by و 'and.' بر شاه is a prepositional phrase 'upon/for the king,' and بد روزگار is an adjectival-nominal phrase 'ill-fated/bad times' (literally 'bad time'), so the clause says that Bayavān and the king are under ill-fortune (the verb 'is/are' is implied).
segments.s246-g185: بدیدار تو is a poetic contraction of بهِ دیدارِ تو (’at seeing you’ / ’to see you’), with the verb شدم (I became) at the end. سخت is an adverbial intensifier modifying شادان (very joyful), and شدم is the past tense/preterite of شدن meaning ‘I became’. Word order is topical object/phrase + adjectival predicate + past auxiliary, typical in Ferdowsi’s style.
segments.s246-g186: ولیکن is a conjunction ‘but / yet’. ز here carries the causal sense ‘because of / on account of’, and introduces the cause phrase ز بیژن غریوان (’on account of Bijan’s bosom/heart’). غریوان functions as a predicative/nominal state tied to the speaker’s feeling, followed by شدم ‘I became’ — i.e. ‘I became (so) in my bosom because of Bijan.’
segments.s246-g187: نبایستمی is an archaic/poetic past-form meaning ‘I should not (have)’ or ‘it was not proper for me’; it expresses regret or reproach. کاین چنین is a fused relative/ demonstrative (که + این چنین) meaning ‘that (you are) thus’ and سوگوار is an adjective ‘mourning / grief‑stricken’ describing the implied subject (you).
segments.s246-g188: ترا دیدمی is ‘I saw you’ (ت = the 2nd‑person element + را = object marker; دیدمی is a past form ‘I saw’). خسته روزگار is a nominal phrase following the object meaning ‘weary/tired of life or fate’ (خسته = tired; روزگار = fate/time). In Persian the adjective phrase follows the object here, completing what was seen.
segments.s246-g189: من از بهر این نامه is a purpose phrase: من ‘I’, از بهر = به‌خاطر/for the sake of, این نامه ‘this letter’. شاه را here precedes the following phrase and uses را as a topical/emphatic marker — it introduces the agent whose order will be mentioned next (i.e. ‘(by) the king’).
segments.s246-g190: بفرمان is an ablative/instrumental phrase ‘by the command (of)’. بسر here corresponds to به‌سر/بسر بردن sense ‘to undergo / to spend / to set out (on a journey)’, and بسپرم (بسپارم) is a 1st‑person present/future form ‘I will entrust / commit’. راه را is the definite object ‘the road / the way’ marked by را. Together: ‘by the king’s order I will entrust myself to the road / set out on the road.’
segments.s246-g191: ز بهر ترا is ‘for your sake’ (ز = because of/for, بهر = for, ترا = you + object marker). خود jگر خسته‌ام places emphasis on ‘myself’ (خود) and then جگر خسته‌ام is an idiom: جگر (‘liver’ = seat of feeling) + خسته‌ام ‘I am weary’ = ‘my heart/liver is tired’ (I am heart‑weary). The predicate خسته‌ام carries the first‑person suffix.
segments.s246-g192: بدین کار is a prepositional phrase بدین = به + این ‘with/for this’, کار ‘task / deed’. بیژن appears as an emphatic apposition or self‑designation (’I, Bijan’). کمر بسته‌ام is a fixed expression ‘I have girded my loins / I have prepared myself’, where بسته‌ام is the perfect/past participial form with the 1st‑person suffix. The clause means ‘for this undertaking I (Bijan) have girded my waist’ (i.e. prepared myself).
segments.s246-g33: This clause lacks the verb (which appears in the next segment). کنون is an adverb 'now'; این یکی is a demonstrative + numeral 'this one'; کار بایسته is a noun + adjective 'a fitting/necessary deed' (adjective follows the noun); پیش is an adverb/prepositional adverb 'before / to the fore' modifying the forthcoming verb.
segments.s246-g34: فراز آمد is a verb phrase ('rose/came up to the height') where فراز precedes آمد to form the motion/image. و simply coordinates clauses. اینت is a poetic/dialectal contraction (این + ت) functioning like 'this is' (i.e. ایـن است). شایسته خوی + ش = شایسته خویش: شایسته 'fitting/appropriate' modifies خویش ('his own'), where خوی + enclitic ش form the possessive.
segments.s246-g35: The order is somewhat inverted: بتو دارد امید گودرز و گیو = 'To you there is hope (held) by Goudarz and Giv' — دادر (دارد) is the present verb 'have/hold' and the agents (گودرز و گیو) follow it. بتو is a prepositional form 'to/for you' and امید is the direct object 'hope'.
segments.s246-g36: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that...'. هستی here expresses the state/existence of the addressee (you are / you will be) used as the predicate. بهر کشور is a purposive prepositional phrase 'for the country', امروز is the time adverb 'today', and نیو (a regional/poetic adjective) modifies the sense 'new' — together: 'that you will be today a new support for the country.'
segments.s246-g37: شناسی here is the 2nd-person verbal form 'you know/recognize' (classical present), followed by بنزدیک من 'near to me' as a locative phrase; جاهشان is a possessed noun 'their rank/status' (جاه + شان). Word order places the predicate 'شناسی' at the front of the clause.
segments.s246-g38: A triadic list linked by و: زبان و دل و رای = 'speech/utterance, heart, and counsel/opinion'. یکتاهشان is یکتاه + شان = 'their oneness/being single' (i.e. united/one‑minded), so the phrase says these three are of one accord.
segments.s246-g39: سزدگر is an evaluative/conditional particle meaning 'it is fitting' or 'if it befit(s)'. It introduces a conditional clause: تو اینرا نداری 'you do not have this' (نداری = 'you lack'). برنج here is the item named ('rice'); the clause says 'if it befits (or if) you lack this rice'.
segments.s246-g40: بخواه is an imperative 'ask/seek' (command/advice). آنچ is a relative/pronominal compound 'that which', followed by باید 'is necessary'; ز مردان و گنج is the source phrase 'from the men and the treasury' (ز is the poetic preposition 'from'). The clause instructs: 'ask from men and the treasure whatever is needed.'
segments.s246-g25: This is a nominal phrase with a relative clause: هران (an archaic form of هر آن, 'each') + بند ('fetter/bond') followed by the relative clause کز دست تو بسته شد. کز is the poetic compound of که + از and here introduces the relative clause 'which/by which'; دست تو (= 'your hand') is the agent and بسته شد is a passive past (بسته + شد) meaning 'was bound/was tied.' Word order is noun + modifying relative clause.
segments.s246-g26: گشایندگان را is topicalized with the object/ topical marker را (literally 'as for the openers'); the predicate is جگر خسته شد, literally 'the liver/heart became tired' — a poetic way to say 'their hearts grew weary.' Note شد here expresses change/becoming ('grew/wascome').
segments.s246-g27: A noun phrase functioning as an epithet: گشاینده ('opener', agent-noun) modifying بند بسته ('the bound/binding bond'). The final توی is an emphatic/poetic second-person enclitic ('you'), placed after the phrase for emphasis (here marking that the described opener is addressed). Note بند بسته is a noun + participial modifier 'the bond that is/was bound.'
segments.s246-g28: This is a verbless equational clause where کیان را marks the possessor/topic ('of the Kayan kings'), سپهر خجسته is the predicate ('auspicious heaven'), and the trailing توی again is the emphatic/poetic 'you' (the one being praised). In classical Persian the structure 'X را Y [تو]' yields 'You are Y of X' (i.e. 'you are X's Y').
segments.s246-g29: ترا (here presented as the separate elements ت + را) is the indirect object 'to you' (حقهٔ مفعولی). ایزد is the subject 'God' and این زور پیلان 'this elephant-like strength' is the direct object; که داد introduces a relative clause 'which gave.' The clause as a whole functions as an optative/wish: 'May God give you...,' a customary poetic prayer — داد is used in a jussive/optative sense ('give').
segments.s246-g30: A simple list of the things 'given' by that strength: دل ('heart'), هوش ('intellect/awareness') and فرهنگ ('culture/learning'). فرخ‌نژاد follows as an epithet/adjective ('well-born' / 'auspicious-born') modifying the preceding noun phrase, so the list names qualities bestowed (or the poet may be invoking a well-born lineage as part of the gift).
segments.s246-g31: بدان داد is 'he gave to that / he granted that' (بـ + دان = 'to that purpose' + داد 'gave'); تا introduces a purpose/result clause ('so that'). دست فریاد خواه is a compact phrase meaning 'the hand of one crying for help' or 'the outstretched hand of a suppliant' (فریادخواه is an agentive/compound meaning 'one who cries for help').
segments.s246-g32: بگیری is subjunctive/imperative 'you may seize/grasp' (the verb is in the subjunctive after تا), followed by برآری 'you may raise/bring up' (برآوردن sense). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (classical ز = از), تاریک چاه 'dark well/pit' is the place from which one is lifted. The pair of verbs (بگیری برآری) expresses taking hold and lifting out.
segments.s246-g249: This is a short verbal phrase with poetic word order: آمد is the past‑tense verb 'came' (3rd sing.), همانگاه is an adverb 'immediately' modifying آمد, and گیو is the subject (proper name) placed after the verb for metrical/poetic effect. بر رستم uses بر as a directional preposition 'toward/to Rostam'.
segments.s246-g250: کز introduces reported content ('that…') and governs the subordinate clause. ایدر is the locative 'here'. نباید is an impersonal modal 'it should not / must not', and شدن follows as an infinitival/impersonal verbal element meaning 'to happen / to be done' (forming 'it must not be that…' or 'one must not…'). پیش is the preposition/adverb 'ahead / before' marking motion or precedence. نیو is uncertain in this clause (archaic/poetical or corrupt in the text) and does not have a clear sense from the given options here.
segments.s246-g251: This clause reports Giv's speech. شوم is a subjunctive/optative form ('may I be / let me…' or here idiomatically 'I will go'); گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (past). آگه کنم is a subordinate verb phrase 'I will inform/notify' with کنم in the present/subjunctive used to express intent or future; شاه را is the definite direct object marked by را ('the king'). Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic rhythm, but the clause expresses 'he said: I will go and inform the king.'
segments.s246-g252: که begins a subordinate clause giving the content of what he will inform the king: a reporting 'that…'. پیمود is the simple past 'traversed' (3rd sing.). رخش is the subject (the famous horse Rakhsh). راه را is the definite object 'the road' with the object marker را. The item تهم in the middle is unclear in this edition (likely a textual or dialectal form) and cannot be matched to the provided senses with confidence.
segments.s246-g253: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. رفت is the past tense 'went / left'. از بر is an idiomatic locative phrase 'from the side/presence of' (از + بر), so از بر رستم = 'from Rostam's side/presence'. پهلوان is the noun 'the champion/hero' (the subject); poetic word order places it after the clause.
segments.s246-g254: بیامد is the past tense 'came' (here describing the champion's movement). بدرگاه is a compound prepositional/locative 'into the presence / to the court of'. شاه جوان is a noun phrase 'the young king' (شاه modified by جوان). The whole segment means 'he came into the young king's presence/court.'
segments.s246-g255: چو again is 'when/as'. نزدیک is 'near/nearby' or 'approaching' and with آمد ('came') gives 'as he came near'. کیخسرو is the proper name (Kay Khosrow). فراز is an adverbial/poetical particle meaning 'forth / up / before (someone)' — here marking approach into the king's presence.
segments.s246-g256: ستودش is the past tense 'praised him' with the enclitic object ـش ('him'). فراوان is an adverb 'greatly / profusely'. و is the coordinating 'and'. بردش نماز is an idiomatic sequence: بردش is a verbal form with enclitic object 'him' (here 'took/received/accepted him' in a ritual or honorific sense) and نماز is 'prayer / respect'; together the phrase conveys that he paid/placed or performed the respectful devotion (i.e., paid his respects). The exact nuance of بردش is somewhat idiomatic/poetic here.
segments.s246-g193: بکوشم is a 1st-person-singular present/subjunctive/hortative form ('I will strive' / 'may I strive'); the prefixed ب- is the old Persian present/subjunctive marker. بدین here is the prepositional phrase 'by/with this' (written in the data as two pieces ב + دین); کارگر functions adjectivally as 'effective' or 'useful' modifying 'کار' (here elliptically 'this work'); جان من is the direct object ('my life'/'my soul'). Word order is Verb — prepositional phrase — adjective — object ('I will strive by/with this effective [work], my life').
segments.s246-g194: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; تن is 'body' and is the source from which separation happens; بگسلد is 3rd-person present/subjunctive or jussive ('may it break/separate' or 'it will be torn away') governing جان من as the thing that separates; پاک here is an epithet 'pure' modifying یزدان 'God'; من is the possessive pronoun 'my'. The clause reads literally 'may my life be torn from the body by my pure God' with the subjunctive/resultive sense in بگسلد.
segments.s246-g195: من is the subject 'I'. از بهر is the set phrase 'for the sake of / on behalf of' (از here contributes the 'because of/for' meaning; بهر = 'for'). ندارم is the present negative 'I do not have / I do not feel (a certain thing)'. برنج in classical/poetic usage here functions idiomatically (something like 'reluctance' or 'shame/hesitation'), but that exact sense is not listed in the lemma data, so its sense is marked -1.
segments.s246-g196: فدا کردن is the light-verb construction 'to sacrifice' (فدا + کردن, where کردن is the auxiliary/light verb). جان و مردان و گنج are coordinated direct objects ('life and men and treasure') linked by و. The list is cumulative objects of 'to sacrifice'.
segments.s246-g197: بنیروی یزدان is an instrumental/prepositional phrase meaning 'by the power of God' (بنیروی = 'by the power/strength of'). ببندم is 1st-person-singular present/subjunctive/hortative of 'بستن' used here as 'to gird' or 'to bind' and takes کمر ('waist, girdle') as its object: 'I will gird my loins.'
segments.s246-g198: This short nominal phrase names the addressee/goal. شهنشاه پیروزگر is a noun phrase 'the Shahanshah, the victorious' where پیروزگر is an epithet ('victorious' / 'triumphant'). ببخت as written is archaic/elliptical here (likely related to به بخت 'with fortune' or an epithet) and does not match the dictionary senses given (which are negative); its function in the line is adverbial/epithetical ('with good fortune' / 'fortunate') and is therefore marked -1 for sense in the provided data.
segments.s246-g199: بیارمش is the verb بیارَ + enclitic object -مش ('I will bring him/it'); the verb is future/intentional 'I will bring (him)'. زان = 'from that' (زان = از + آن). بند تاریک چاه is a genitive/attributive chain: 'the fetter/bond of the dark pit' or more loosely 'that dark fettered pit' (بند = fetter/bond, تاریک = dark, چاه = pit/well).
segments.s246-g200: نشانمش is نشانَ + enclitic -مش 'I will show him/it' (verb with object enclitic). با شاه در پیشگاه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of the king / before the king at court' (با = 'with/in the presence of', در پیشگاه = 'in the presence / at the court'). Together: 'I will show him to the king in the court.'
segments.s246-g57: چنانچون introduces a simile/comparison ('just as'). بباید is the modal 'ought/must' and is followed by the subjunctive/optative verb بسازی (2nd person singular 'you compose'), with نوا as the direct object 'melody' placed after the verb in poetic word order.
segments.s246-g58: مگر here functions as 'unless' (an exception). بیژن is the subject. از بند is a prepositional phrase 'from the bond(s)/chains' and یابد is a 3rd-person present/subjunctive 'finds/obtains'; رها is a predicative adjective 'free' that completes یابد (poetic inversion places یابد before رها).
segments.s246-g59: چو = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. برنامه بنهاد is a verb phrase 'he set/laid the plan' (بنهاد = past 3rd sg.), and خسرو (the agent) appears after the verb by poetic inversion; نگین (the gem/signet) is placed here as the topical object that links to the next clause.
segments.s246-g60: بشد is the past/ perfective sense of شدن 'became/was made' (here: 'was fashioned/it happened'); گیو is the following subject by inversion. و links the clauses, and بر شاه کرد آفرین is a verb phrase 'he made praise upon the king' (کرد آفرین = 'praised/applauded the king').
segments.s246-g61: سواران 'horsemen/riders' is the noun phrase; دوده is an attributive epithet ('soot-/dust-covered') describing the riders. همه 'all' emphasizes the plurality, and برنشاند (past 3rd sg. or perfective) is a transitive verb 'set/placed/presented' — here 'he marshaled/posted' them, with the agent implied from previous context.
segments.s246-g62: بیزدان is used as a substantive 'the godless (ones)'. پناهید is read as a perfective/preterite 'they sought refuge' (subject understood from context). و links to the final clause; لشکر is the subject 'the army' and براند (3rd-person) is 'drives/ drove away' (transitive), here 'the army drove them off.'
segments.s246-g63: چو = 'when' (temporal). نخجیر here means 'the hunt / hunting expedition' and از آنجا = 'from that place.' که introduces the subordinate clause and برداشتی is 2nd-person past 'you took/removed'—together: 'when you took the hunt from that place.'
segments.s246-g64: دو روزه literally 'two-day (period)' and بیک روزه here functions as 'into one day' (بِ + یک contracted in poetry), so the phrase reports compressing 'two days into one.' بگذاشتی is 2nd-person past ('you made/you left/you caused') used here in the sense 'you reduced/made into.' Note the lexicon items are slightly fused in the manuscript; grammatically this is a comparative/compressive statement.
